topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Owner's Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Owner's Specifications** are the comprehensive set of operational, functional, economic, and regulatory requirements formally communicated by the shipowner (or operating company) to the naval architect at the inception of a fishing vessel design project. These specifications form the **primary input vector** to the design spiral and act as the binding performance contract between the owner and the design team.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, owner's specifications constitute the **first node of the design spiral** (Watson & Gilfillan formulation), encapsulating qualitative desires and quantitative performance targets that the final vessel must satisfy.

## 1.2 Components of Owner's Specifications

A complete owner's specification document generally contains the following parameters:

| # | Parameter Category | Typical Examples |
|---|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Mission Profile** | Type of fishing (trawling, purse seining, longlining, gillnetting) |
| 2 | **Operational Range** | Endurance at sea (e.g., 10–30 days) |
| 3 | **Catch Capacity** | Fish hold volume in cubic meters (m³) |
| 4 | **Service Speed** | Vessel speed at design draught (knots) |
| 5 | **Propulsion Plant** | Engine type, power, fuel type |
| 6 | **Crew Complement** | Number of crew, accommodation standards |
| 7 | **Fishing Gear** | Winches, net drums, cranes capacity |
| 8 | **Economic Targets** | Capital cost ceiling, expected ROI period |
| 9 | **Regulatory Compliance** | IMO, Indian Fisheries Act, ILO 188, classification society rules |
| 10 | **Geographic Limits** | Coastal, EEZ, deep-sea operation zones |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Building a Custom House**
> Think of designing a fishing vessel exactly like commissioning a custom-built house. Before the architect draws a single line, the homeowner submits a "brief" — number of bedrooms, budget ceiling, plot size constraints, Vaastu preferences, and timeline. The architect cannot start the blueprint without this brief. **Owner's Specifications are this brief to the naval architect.** If the brief is vague, the design oscillates repeatedly in the design spiral (costing money and time). A precise brief accelerates convergence to the optimal vessel.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard metrics commonly appearing in owner's specifications:
> - **Service Speed ($V_s$):** typically **9–14 knots** for small/medium fishing vessels
> - **Block Coefficient ($C_B$):** typically **0.55–0.72** for displacement hulls
> - **Endurance:** measured in **days** at sea
> - **Hold Capacity:** measured in **m³** or **tonnes** of catch
> - **Deadweight:** measured in **tonnes (T)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Owner's Specification as Input to the Design Spiral

The design of a fishing vessel is an iterative process represented by the **Design Spiral** (originated by J.H. Watson and refined by A.W. Gilfillan). The owner's specifications act as the **boundary condition** for this spiral.

### 2.1.1 Logical Steps in Owner's Specification Formulation

1. **Step 1 — Mission Definition:** The owner defines what the vessel must *do* (e.g., shrimp trawling in the Arabian Sea).
2. **Step 2 — Performance Targets:** Quantitative goals such as speed, range, and payload are set.
3. **Step 3 — Constraint Identification:** Limitations on length, draft (harbour depth), cost, and crew size are imposed.
4. **Step 4 — Economic Envelope:** Capital expenditure (CAPEX) ceiling and operational expenditure (OPEX) targets are established.
5. **Step 5 — Regulatory Binding:** Compliance with statutory bodies (Maritime Board, Classification Society, FAO Code of Conduct) is locked in.
6. **Step 6 — Documentation:** All parameters are recorded in the **Specification Document**, which becomes the yardstick of design acceptance.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Specification Parameter

> [!NOTE]
> **Mission Profile** → dictates hull form, gear arrangement, freeboard.
> **Service Speed** → directly determines installed engine power via the **Admiralty Coefficient** or **Holtrop resistance estimates**.
> **Catch Capacity** → fixes minimum fish-hold volume and hence influences Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP).
> **Economic Targets** → governs choice of steel grade, outfitting level, and propulsion redundancy.
> **Regulatory Compliance** → non-negotiable; failure here invalidates the design irrespective of other merits.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a high-yield reference for problems that link owner's specifications to principal dimensions.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $V_s = f(L, B, T, C_B, P)$ | Service speed is a function of principal dimensions and power | $V_s$ in knots |
| 2 | $P_E = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V_s}{\eta_D \cdot \eta_S}$ | Effective Power from Total Resistance | $P_E$ in kW, $R_T$ in kN, $V_s$ in m/s |
| 3 | $C_{Adm} = \dfrac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V_s^3}{P_E}$ | Admiralty Coefficient (typically 250–450 for trawlers) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $DWT = W_{catch} + W_{fuel} + W_{crew} + W_{stores}$ | Deadweight composition | in tonnes |
| 5 | $LBP_{approx} \approx k \cdot (V_s)^{0.5}$ | Empirical LBP-speed relation | $k$ empirical constant |
| 6 | $V_{hold} = \dfrac{W_{catch}}{\rho_{fish} \cdot S_F}$ | Minimum fish-hold volume | $V_{hold}$ in m³, $S_F$ stowage factor (m³/t) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stowage Factor ($S_F$):** Volume occupied per tonne of fish. For example, iced fish in bulk has $S_F \approx 1.5$–$2.0 \text{ m}^3/\text{t}$, while frozen block stowed fish is $1.2$–$1.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{t}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

In the Indian fishing industry, owner's specifications for vessels under the **Pradhan Mantri Matsya Sampada Yojana (PMMSY)** subsidization scheme must conform to:
- Indian Register of Shipping (IRS) rules for vessels above 24 m.
- State Maritime Board regulations for smaller crafts.
- ILO Convention 188 (Work in Fishing) for crew conditions.

Failure to correctly capture owner's requirements leads to:
- Costly redesign cycles.
- Vessels underpowered for the assigned gear.
- Non-compliance penalties and licence cancellation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Linking Owner's Specifications to Principal Dimensions

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Demonstrate how a single owner-specified parameter (catch capacity) is mathematically converted into a principal dimension (Length Between Perpendiculars, LBP).

### 3.1.1 Given Owner Specifications

> Service speed: $V_s = 10$ knots
> Catch per voyage: $W_{catch} = 25$ tonnes
> Endurance: $E = 15$ days
> Stowage factor: $S_F = 1.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{t}$
> Block coefficient assumption: $C_B = 0.62$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Calculate Minimum Hold Volume ($V_{hold}$):**

$$
V_{hold} = W_{catch} \times S_F
$$

$$
V_{hold} = 25 \times 1.6 = 40 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Estimate Total Deadweight (DWT):**

$$
DWT = W_{catch} + W_{fuel} + W_{crew} + W_{stores}
$$

Assuming for a small trawler of this size:
- Fuel for 15 days at 10 knots ≈ $W_{fuel} = 8$ t
- Crew + provisions ≈ $W_{crew} = 2$ t
- Stores (gear, ice, salt) ≈ $W_{stores} = 5$ t

$$
DWT = 25 + 8 + 2 + 5 = 40 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 3 — Approximate Displacement ($\Delta$):**

For a fishing vessel, lightweight $\approx 0.55 \times DWT$ to $0.70 \times DWT$ (empirical).

$$
\Delta = DWT + W_{light}
$$

$$
\Delta = 40 + (0.65 \times 40) = 40 + 26 = 66 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Volume Displacement Equation to Estimate LBP:**

The volumetric relationship:

$$
\Delta = \rho \cdot LBP \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B
$$

where seawater density $\rho = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$.

Assume for a small fishing trawler the standard proportions:
- $B/LBP = 0.30$
- $T/LBP = 0.10$

$$
B = 0.30 \cdot LBP, \quad T = 0.10 \cdot LBP
$$

Substituting:

$$
\Delta = 1.025 \cdot LBP \cdot (0.30 \cdot LBP) \cdot (0.10 \cdot LBP) \cdot 0.62
$$

$$
\Delta = 1.025 \cdot 0.0186 \cdot LBP^3
$$

$$
\Delta = 0.01907 \cdot LBP^3
$$

Solving for LBP:

$$
LBP^3 = \dfrac{\Delta}{0.01907}
$$

$$
LBP^3 = \dfrac{66}{0.01907}
$$

$$
LBP^3 \approx 3460 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
LBP \approx \sqrt[3]{3460} \approx 15.1 \text{ m}
$$

### 3.1.3 Final Result Verification

$$
LBP \approx 15.1 \text{ m}, \quad B \approx 4.53 \text{ m}, \quad T \approx 1.51 \text{ m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This LBP estimate (~15 m) is consistent with a 15–18 m small fishing trawler operating in coastal Indian waters — confirming that the owner's catch-capacity specification translates into a dimensionally realistic hull.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Translation of Owner's Specifications to Design Variables

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix demonstrates the **causal mapping** between owner-specified parameters and downstream design variables. This is a high-yield KTU question pattern.

| Owner's Specification | Downstream Design Variable(s) | Governing Equation / Rule |
|------------------------|--------------------------------|---------------------------|
| Service Speed ($V_s$) | Installed Power ($P$) | $P = f(R_T, V_s, \eta_D)$ |
| Catch Capacity | Fish-hold volume → LBP | $V_{hold} = W_{catch} \cdot S_F$ |
| Endurance | Fuel capacity → Range | $R = V_s \cdot E \cdot 24$ |
| Harbour Restriction | Maximum Length Overall (LOA), Beam (B) | $LOA \le L_{harbour}$ |
| Crew Complement | Accommodation block volume | ILO 188 standards |
| Type of Fishing Gear | Deck machinery capacity, freeboard | Gear-specific standards |
| Capital Cost Ceiling | Steel weight, outfitting grade | Cost–weight trade-off |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Owner's Specifications as Input Node of the Design Spiral

The following Mermaid block illustrates how owner's specifications integrate into the **Watson–Gilfillan Design Spiral** and how they cascade through the design process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Owner Specifications Input] --> B[Mission Profile Definition]
    A --> C[Performance Targets Speed Range]
    A --> D[Economic Constraints CAPEX OPEX]
    A --> E[Regulatory Compliance IRS ILO 188]
    
    B --> F[Conceptual Design Phase]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    
    F --> G[Estimate Principal Dimensions LBP B T]
    G --> H[Estimate Power Requirement]
    H --> I[Weight Estimation Deadweight Lightweight]
    I --> J[Stability and Trim Check]
    J --> K{Specifications Satisfied?}
    
    K -- Yes --> L[Final Design Output]
    K -- No --> F
    
    D --> M[Cost Optimisation Loop]
    M --> F
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Owner's Specification Subsystems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OperationalRequirements[Operational Requirements Subsystem]
        OP1[Mission Profile]
        OP2[Service Speed]
        OP3[Endurance]
        OP4[Catch Capacity]
    end
    
    subgraph EconomicConstraints[Economic Constraints Subsystem]
        EC1[Capital Cost Ceiling]
        EC2[Operating Cost Target]
        EC3[ROI Period]
    end
    
    subgraph RegulatoryCompliance[Regulatory Compliance Subsystem]
        RC1[Classification Society Rules]
        RC2[ILO 188 Crew Standards]
        RC3[Coastal State Regulations]
    end
    
    subgraph GeometricConstraints[Geometric Constraints Subsystem]
        GC1[Harbour Depth Limit]
        GC2[Maximum LOA]
        GC3[Maximum Beam]
    end
    
    OperationalRequirements --> DESCON[Design Synthesis Engine]
    EconomicConstraints --> DESCON
    RegulatoryCompliance --> DESCON
    GeometricConstraints --> DESCON
    DESCON --> PRINCIPAL[Principal Dimensions LBP B T]
    DESCON --> POWER[Installed Power kW]
    DESCON --> STAB[Stability Compliance]
```

## 4.3 Iteration Sequence in Design Spiral Driven by Owner Specs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Read Owner Specs] --> S2[Step 2 Assume LBP B T from Std Series]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Compute Resistance R_T]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Compute EHP from R_T]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Compute SHP applying propulsive coeff]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Compare SHP with Owner Specified Speed Power Target]
    S6 --> S7{Match Achieved?}
    S7 -- No --> S8[Adjust LBP B T and Repeat]
    S8 --> S2
    S7 -- Yes --> S9[Proceed to Weight and Stability Iteration]
    S9 --> S10[Final Design Locked]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

### Question 1
**Define Owner's Specifications in the context of fishing vessel design. List any four key parameters it must contain.**

**Model Answer:**
Owner's specifications are the comprehensive set of operational, performance, economic, and regulatory requirements formally provided by the vessel owner to the naval architect at the start of the design process. They act as the primary input to the design spiral.

Four key parameters:
1. **Mission Profile** (type of fishing operation).
2. **Service Speed** (typically 9–14 knots).
3. **Catch Capacity / Fish-hold volume** (in m³ or tonnes).
4. **Economic Targets** (capital cost ceiling and ROI period).

*(Additional valid points: endurance, crew complement, regulatory compliance, harbour constraints, fishing gear type.)*

---

### Question 2
**Why is the mission profile considered the most fundamental component of the owner's specifications?**

**Model Answer:**
The mission profile dictates the **entire downstream design** — hull form, propulsion choice, deck machinery, freeboard, and stability characteristics. A vessel designed for shrimp trawling in coastal waters has fundamentally different requirements from one designed for tuna longlining in deep-sea operations. Without a clearly defined mission, every subsequent design decision becomes ambiguous, leading to iterations in the design spiral and wastage of design effort.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]`

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various components that constitute a complete owner's specification document for a deep-sea fishing vessel. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An owner specifies a fishing trawler with the following requirements: Catch per voyage = 30 tonnes; Stowage factor = 1.5 m³/t; Service speed = 11 knots; Endurance = 20 days; Block coefficient = 0.60. Estimate the **minimum fish-hold volume** and the **approximate LBP**, assuming standard proportions B/LBP = 0.32 and T/LBP = 0.11. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

A complete owner's specification document for a deep-sea fishing vessel comprises:

1. **Mission Profile:** Type of fishery (e.g., tuna longlining, squid jigging), target species, operating zone.
2. **Performance Targets:** Service speed, bollard pull, cruising range.
3. **Capacity Requirements:** Fish-hold volume, fuel oil capacity, freshwater capacity, cargo/equipment stowage.
4. **Propulsion Specifications:** Engine type (diesel), power rating, fuel grade, redundancy requirement.
5. **Fishing Gear and Deck Machinery:** Winch pull, drum capacity, crane SWL, gantry dimensions.
6. **Crew Accommodation:** Number of crew, cabin standards (per ILO 188), galley, mess, sanitary facilities.
7. **Economic Constraints:** Maximum allowable capital cost, target payback period, expected annual revenue.
8. **Regulatory Requirements:** Classification society (IRS / DNV / ABS), flag state rules, MARPOL compliance, IMO conventions.
9. **Geographic and Harbour Constraints:** Maximum LOA, beam, and draft allowed by intended ports.
10. **Operational Endurance:** Number of days at sea without refuelling.
11. **Quality and Material Standards:** Steel grade, corrosion protection, hull paint system.
12. **Maintainability and Spare Parts Policy.**

Each of these components feeds into specific design variables in the Watson-Gilfillan design spiral.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given Data:**
- $W_{catch} = 30$ t
- $S_F = 1.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{t}$
- $V_s = 11$ knots
- $E = 20$ days
- $C_B = 0.60$
- $B/LBP = 0.32$, $T/LBP = 0.11$
- $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Minimum Fish-hold Volume:**

$$
V_{hold} = W_{catch} \times S_F
$$

$$
V_{hold} = 30 \times 1.5 = 45 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Estimate Total Deadweight:**

- Fuel for 20 days at 11 knots ≈ 12 t
- Crew + provisions ≈ 3 t
- Stores + ice + gear ≈ 8 t

$$
DWT = 30 + 12 + 3 + 8 = 53 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Displacement:**

Lightweight ≈ $0.65 \times DWT = 0.65 \times 53 = 34.45$ t

$$
\Delta = DWT + W_{light} = 53 + 34.45 \approx 87.5 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Volumetric Equation:**

$$
\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot LBP \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B
$$

With $B = 0.32 \cdot LBP$, $T = 0.11 \cdot LBP$:

$$
\Delta = 1.025 \cdot LBP \cdot (0.32 \cdot LBP) \cdot (0.11 \cdot LBP) \cdot 0.60
$$

$$
\Delta = 1.025 \cdot 0.02112 \cdot LBP^3
$$

$$
\Delta = 0.02165 \cdot LBP^3
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for LBP:**

$$
LBP^3 = \dfrac{87.5}{0.02165} \approx 4042
$$

$$
LBP \approx \sqrt[3]{4042} \approx 15.94 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Result Verification:**

$$
LBP \approx 15.9 \text{ m}, \quad B \approx 5.1 \text{ m}, \quad T \approx 1.75 \text{ m}
$$

> The vessel is dimensionally realistic for a small-to-medium deep-sea trawler in the 15–18 m class.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the role of **economic considerations** in shaping the owner's specifications of a fishing vessel. How do CAPEX, OPEX, and ROI targets influence the design spiral? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fishing vessel owner wants a small gillnetter with the following specs: Service speed = 9 knots; Fish hold = 20 m³; Crew = 8; Endurance = 7 days. Estimate the **approximate LBP and required shaft power**, assuming $C_B = 0.58$, $B/LBP = 0.28$, $T/LBP = 0.09$, total resistance coefficient $C_T = 0.005$ at the design speed, and overall propulsive efficiency $\eta_D = 0.55$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

Economic considerations are non-negotiable elements of owner's specifications and act as a **filter** on every technical decision:

1. **CAPEX (Capital Expenditure):** The owner's maximum investment ceiling restricts choice of:
   - Steel weight and grade (higher tensile steel is costlier).
   - Engine make and brand (Japanese vs. Chinese vs. Indian).
   - Outfitting sophistication (electronics, automation level).
   - Hull finish (blast and paint quality).

2. **OPEX (Operating Expenditure):** Determines:
   - Choice of fuel-efficient hull forms (high $C_B$ for displacement mode).
   - Crew wages and skill level (smaller crew = lower OPEX but requires automation).
   - Maintenance philosophy (planned maintenance vs. breakdown maintenance).

3. **ROI Target (Return on Investment):** Typical fishing vessels target ROI in **5–8 years**. This forces:
   - Maximisation of operational days (endurance and reliability).
   - Minimisation of fuel consumption.
   - Selection of gear with high catch-per-unit-effort (CPUE).

4. **Influence on the Design Spiral:** Economics drives the convergence point. If the owner-specified power and speed push SHP above the budget, the design spiral must be re-iterated with reduced speed target or revised LBP — directly linking technical design back to owner's economic envelope.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:**
- $V_s = 9$ knots = $9 \times 0.5144 = 4.63$ m/s
- $V_{hold} = 20$ m³ → $W_{catch} = 20 / 1.6 = 12.5$ t (assuming $S_F = 1.6$)
- Crew = 8 → $W_{crew} \approx 1.6$ t (with provisions)
- Endurance = 7 days → $W_{fuel} \approx 2.5$ t
- $W_{stores} \approx 1.5$ t

**Step 1 — Deadweight & Displacement:**

$$
DWT = 12.5 + 2.5 + 1.6 + 1.5 = 18.1 \text{ t}
$$

$$
W_{light} \approx 0.70 \times DWT = 12.7 \text{ t}
$$

$$
\Delta \approx 30.8 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve LBP:**

$$
\Delta = 1.025 \cdot LBP^3 \cdot (0.28)(0.09)(0.58)
$$

$$
\Delta = 1.025 \cdot 0.01462 \cdot LBP^3 = 0.01498 \cdot LBP^3
$$

$$
LBP^3 = 30.8 / 0.01498 \approx 2056
$$

$$
LBP \approx 12.7 \text{ m}, \quad B \approx 3.55 \text{ m}, \quad T \approx 1.14 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Estimate Total Resistance:**

Using ITTC-style friction-resistance form with a single coefficient $C_T$:

$$
R_T = C_T \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot S \cdot V_s^2
$$

where $S$ (wetted surface area) is estimated using the Holtrop approximation or simplified as:

$$
S \approx 2.5 \cdot \sqrt{\Delta \cdot LBP} \approx 2.5 \cdot \sqrt{30.8 \cdot 12.7} \approx 49.4 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
R_T = 0.005 \cdot 1025 \cdot 49.4 \cdot (4.63)^2
$$

$$
R_T = 0.005 \cdot 1025 \cdot 49.4 \cdot 21.44
$$

$$
R_T \approx 5428 \text{ N} \approx 5.43 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Effective Power:**

$$
P_E = R_T \cdot V_s = 5428 \times 4.63 = 25,131 \text{ W} \approx 25.1 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Shaft Power (Brake Power):**

$$
P_B = \dfrac{P_E}{\eta_D} = \dfrac{25.1}{0.55} \approx 45.6 \text{ kW} \approx 61 \text{ hp}
$$

> The required shaft power for this gillnetter is approximately **45–50 kW** (~60 hp), consistent with auxiliary-engine installations typical of small Indian coastal fishing gillnetters.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in Owner's Specification questions (frequently penalized in KTU valuations):**
> 1. **Confusing "Service Speed" with "Trial Speed":** Service speed is the sustained speed at design draught with 85% MCR; trial speed is the maximum speed measured during sea trials at light load. Mixing these gives wrong power estimates.
> 2. **Forgetting to include crew weight in DWT:** Many students compute only catch weight and miss the fuel, crew, stores, and freshwater weights — leading to underestimation of displacement.
> 3. **Using freshwater density instead of seawater density:** $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$ must be used for displacement calculations; freshwater is 1.000 t/m³.
> 4. **Ignoring the lightweight-to-DWT ratio:** Hard-coding 0.50 or 0.55 without justification. The correct empirical value depends on vessel size (0.65 for small trawlers, 0.50 for large seiners).
> 5. **Skipping units in the final answer:** Always state LBP in metres, $V_{hold}$ in m³, and power in kW (with hp in parentheses for industry familiarity).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Owner's Specifications**
> - Owner's Specifications = **primary input to the Watson-Gilfillan Design Spiral**.
> - Components: mission profile, performance targets, capacity, endurance, economic envelope, regulatory compliance, geometric constraints.
> - Mission Profile is the **most fundamental** parameter — it dictates hull form and machinery.
> - Catch capacity translates to **fish-hold volume** using the **stowage factor** ($S_F$).
> - Service speed + total resistance + propulsive efficiency → **shaft power**.
> - Deadweight = $W_{catch} + W_{fuel} + W_{crew} + W_{stores}$.
> - Total displacement $\Delta = DWT + W_{light}$, with $W_{light} \approx 0.55$–$0.70 \times DWT$ for fishing vessels.
> - Volume displacement equation: $\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot LBP \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B$.
> - Standard proportions for small fishing trawlers: $B/LBP \approx 0.28$–$0.32$; $T/LBP \approx 0.09$–$0.11$.
> - Block coefficient for displacement fishing vessels: $C_B \approx 0.55$–$0.72$.
> - Indian regulatory references: IRS rules, ILO 188, State Maritime Board, PMMSY norms.
> - A precise owner's specification **shortens the design spiral** and reduces cost overruns.
> - Service speed ($V_s$) for small fishing vessels: typically **9–14 knots**.
> - Admiralty Coefficient range for trawlers: **250–450** (dimensionless).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Economy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Fishing Vessel Design**, **Economy** refers to the systematic economic analysis of the vessel throughout its operational lifecycle, encompassing **capital investment**, **operational expenditure**, **revenue generation**, and **profitability indicators**. It is the engineering-economics discipline that balances **technical performance** with **financial viability**, ensuring that the designed vessel achieves an optimal trade-off between **catch capacity**, **fuel efficiency**, **crew comfort**, and **return on investment (ROI)**.

The economy of a fishing vessel is evaluated through several quantitative parameters:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Economic Metrics in Fishing Vessel Design**
> - **Capital Cost ($C_{cap}$)** – Initial investment for construction
> - **Annual Operating Cost ($C_{op}$)** – Recurring yearly expenses
> - **Annual Revenue ($R$)** – Income from fish catch sales
> - **Net Annual Profit ($P$)** – Revenue minus all costs
> - **Payback Period ($T_p$)** – Time to recover initial investment
> - **Net Present Value (NPV)** – Discounted lifetime profit
> - **Cost per Tonne of Catch ($C_{pt}$)** – Operational efficiency metric

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are buying an **auto-rickshaw** to earn a livelihood. Before purchasing, you don't just look at the price tag — you think about:

- **How much fuel does it consume per day?** (Operating cost)
- **How many passengers can it carry?** (Capacity → Revenue)
- **How often does it need servicing?** (Maintenance cost)
- **How many years will it run before becoming uneconomical?** (Economic life)
- **After how many years will you recover the down-payment?** (Payback period)

**A fishing vessel is exactly the same — but on a much larger scale.** Instead of passengers, the "cargo" is fish. Instead of city roads, the "route" is the open sea. The owner (fisherman or company) must ensure that the money spent on building and running the vessel is **less than** the money earned from selling the catch — otherwise, the venture is a loss.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of Fishing Vessel Economy**
> $$\boxed{\text{Vessel is Economically Viable} \iff \text{Total Revenue} > \text{Total Cost (Capital + Operating)}}$$

### 1.3 Why Economy is Critical in Design

Every design decision — from **hull form** to **engine selection**, from **freezer capacity** to **crew quarters** — has a direct economic impact:

| Design Choice | Economic Consequence |
|---|---|
| Larger hull | Higher building cost, more fuel, but greater catch capacity |
| Bigger engine | Faster cruising, higher fuel cost |
| Onboard freezing | Higher capital cost, but longer trips → more revenue |
| Better hull coating | Higher initial cost, lower maintenance and fuel use |

A designer who ignores economy builds a vessel that is **technically perfect but financially bankrupt**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of Fishing Vessel Economy

The economy of a fishing vessel is built upon **three pillars**:

#### **Pillar 1: Capital Cost (One-time Investment)**

This is the **upfront cost** of building the vessel. It includes:

- **Hull construction cost** ($C_{hull}$) — steel, aluminum, or FRP
- **Main engine and propulsion** ($C_{prop}$)
- **Auxiliary machinery** ($C_{aux}$) — generators, compressors, pumps
- **Fishing gear and equipment** ($C_{gear}$) — nets, winches, lines
- **Refrigeration and fish handling** ($C_{ref}$)
- **Electronics and navigation** ($C_{elec}$)
- **Outfitting and accommodation** ($C_{out}$)

$$\boxed{C_{cap} = C_{hull} + C_{prop} + C_{aux} + C_{gear} + C_{ref} + C_{elec} + C_{out}}$$

#### **Pillar 2: Annual Operating Cost (Recurring Expense)**

This is incurred **every year** the vessel operates:

- **Crew wages and benefits** ($C_{crew}$)
- **Fuel and lubricants** ($C_{fuel}$)
- **Maintenance and repairs** ($C_{maint}$)
- **Insurance** ($C_{ins}$)
- **Port charges and harbor dues** ($C_{port}$)
- **Bait, ice, and consumables** ($C_{cons}$)
- **Depreciation** ($D$) — non-cash but accounting cost
- **Interest on loan** ($C_{int}$)

$$\boxed{C_{op} = C_{crew} + C_{fuel} + C_{maint} + C_{ins} + C_{port} + C_{cons} + D + C_{int}}$$

#### **Pillar 3: Annual Revenue (Income)**

The vessel earns through:

- **Sale of fish catch** — by weight (kg/tonne) × market price per kg
- **By-products** — fish meal, oil, shells
- **Charter or lease income** (occasionally)

$$R = Q \times p$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Annual catch quantity (tonnes)
- $p$ = Average market price per tonne (currency)

### 2.2 Depreciation — The Hidden Cost

**Depreciation** is the reduction in the vessel's value over time due to wear, tear, and obsolescence. Two common methods:

#### **Method 1: Straight-Line Depreciation**

$$D = \frac{C_{cap} - S}{L}$$

Where:
- $S$ = **Scrap/Salvage value** at end of life
- $L$ = **Economic life** of vessel (years)

#### **Method 2: Declining Balance Depreciation**

$$D_t = C_{cap} \times (1 - r)^{t-1} \times r$$

Where:
- $r$ = **Depreciation rate** (fraction)
- $t$ = **Year number**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are essential for KTU ESE questions on Economy.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{cap} = \sum C_i$ | Total capital cost | Currency |
| 2 | $C_{op} = C_{crew} + C_{fuel} + C_{maint} + \cdots$ | Annual operating cost | Currency/year |
| 3 | $R = Q \times p$ | Annual revenue | Currency/year |
| 4 | $P = R - C_{op}$ | Annual net profit | Currency/year |
| 5 | $D = \dfrac{C_{cap} - S}{L}$ | Straight-line depreciation | Currency/year |
| 6 | $D_t = C_{cap}(1-r)^{t-1} r$ | Declining balance depreciation | Currency/year |
| 7 | $T_p = \dfrac{C_{cap}}{P}$ | Payback period | Years |
| 8 | $C_{pt} = \dfrac{C_{op}}{Q}$ | Cost per tonne of catch | Currency/tonne |
| 9 | $ROI = \dfrac{P}{C_{cap}} \times 100$ | Return on Investment | Percentage |
| 10 | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{L} \dfrac{P_t}{(1+i)^t} - C_{cap}$ | Net Present Value | Currency |
| 11 | $BEP = \dfrac{C_{fix}}{p - C_{var}}$ | Break-even catch quantity | Tonnes |
| 12 | $\eta_{econ} = \dfrac{R - C_{op}}{R} \times 100$ | Economic efficiency | Percentage |

> **Note on notation used above:** $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value when needed in problems, and $i$ is the **discount/interest rate** (e.g., 0.08 for 8%).

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

These formulas are used by:

- **Shipyards** to quote building costs and profit margins
- **Fishing companies** to decide whether to build, buy, or charter a vessel
- **Banks** to evaluate loan applications for vessel construction
- **Government subsidy departments** (e.g., MPEDA, NFDB in India) to grant aid
- **Insurance companies** to compute premiums based on vessel value
- **Classification societies** to certify economic fitness
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Payback Period

The **Payback Period ($T_p$)** is the time required for cumulative net profit to equal the initial capital investment.

**Step 1: Define cumulative profit at end of year $t$.**

Assuming annual profit $P$ is constant:

$$\text{Cumulative Profit}(t) = P \times t$$

**Step 2: Set cumulative profit equal to capital cost.**

At payback, the owner has recovered the initial investment exactly:

$$P \times T_p = C_{cap}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $T_p$.**

$$T_p = \frac{C_{cap}}{P}$$

**Interpretation:**

- If $T_p < L$ (vessel's economic life), the investment is **profitable**.
- If $T_p > L$, the investment is a **loss**.
- If $T_p = L$, the owner **breaks even** (no profit, no loss).

**Numerical Example:**

Suppose a fishing trawler costs **₹ 2 Crore** to build and earns an annual net profit of **₹ 40 Lakh**.

$$T_p = \frac{2{,}00{,}00{,}000}{40{,}00{,}000} = 5 \text{ years}$$

If the vessel's economic life is **15 years**, the owner recovers investment in 5 years and enjoys **10 years of pure profit**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Net Present Value (NPV)

Money received in the future is worth **less** than money received today (because of inflation and opportunity cost). NPV accounts for this.

**Step 1: Define discount factor.**

A sum of ₹1 received after $t$ years at discount rate $i$ is worth today:

$$\text{Present Value} = \frac{1}{(1+i)^t}$$

**Step 2: Define annual discounted cash flow.**

If profit in year $t$ is $P_t$, its present value is:

$$PV_t = \frac{P_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

**Step 3: Sum over entire economic life.**

$$\text{Total Present Value of Profits} = \sum_{t=1}^{L} \frac{P_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

**Step 4: Subtract initial capital cost.**

$$\boxed{NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{L} \frac{P_t}{(1+i)^t} - C_{cap}}$$

**Decision Rule:**

$$\begin{aligned}
NPV > 0 &\Rightarrow \text{Profitable — accept the project} \\
NPV = 0 &\Rightarrow \text{Indifferent} \\
NPV < 0 &\Rightarrow \text{Loss-making — reject the project}
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical Example:**

A purse seiner costs ₹5 Crore. Annual profit for 10 years is ₹80 Lakh. Discount rate is 8%.

**Step-by-step calculation:**

Assume constant profit $P = 80$ Lakhs. We need:

$$PV_{factor} = \sum_{t=1}^{10} \frac{1}{(1.08)^t}$$

Using the geometric series sum formula:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{1}{(1+i)^t} = \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$$

$$\sum_{t=1}^{10} \frac{1}{(1.08)^t} = \frac{1 - (1.08)^{-10}}{0.08}$$

Compute $(1.08)^{10}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.08)^2 &= 1.1664 \\
(1.08)^4 &= (1.1664)^2 = 1.36049 \\
(1.08)^5 &= 1.36049 \times 1.08 = 1.46933 \\
(1.08)^{10} &= (1.46933)^2 = 2.15892
\end{aligned}$$

So $(1.08)^{-10} = 1 / 2.15892 = 0.46319$

$$\sum_{t=1}^{10} \frac{1}{(1.08)^t} = \frac{1 - 0.46319}{0.08} = \frac{0.53681}{0.08} = 6.7101$$

Present value of total profits:

$$PV = 80{,}00{,}000 \times 6.7101 = 5{,}36{,}80{,}800$$

NPV:

$$NPV = 5{,}36{,}80{,}800 - 5{,}00{,}00{,}000 = 36{,}80{,}800$$

Since $NPV > 0$, the project is **profitable**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Cost per Tonne of Catch

This is the **operational efficiency metric** — lower is better.

**Step 1: Define annual operating cost.**

$$C_{op} = \sum (\text{all recurring expenses})$$

**Step 2: Define annual catch.**

$$Q = \text{Average annual catch in tonnes}$$

**Step 3: Form the ratio.**

$$\boxed{C_{pt} = \frac{C_{op}}{Q} \quad \text{[Currency per tonne]}}$$

**Insight:** A well-designed vessel has lower $C_{pt}$ because it catches more fish per rupee spent.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Break-Even Catch Quantity

The vessel **breaks even** when revenue equals total cost:

$$R = C_{fix} + C_{var} \cdot Q$$

Where:
- $C_{fix}$ = Fixed costs (depreciation, insurance, crew salaries)
- $C_{var}$ = Variable cost per tonne (fuel, ice, bait)

At break-even, $R = p \cdot Q$ where $p$ is the price per tonne:

$$p \cdot Q_{BE} = C_{fix} + C_{var} \cdot Q_{BE}$$

Rearrange:

$$p \cdot Q_{BE} - C_{var} \cdot Q_{BE} = C_{fix}$$

$$Q_{BE} (p - C_{var}) = C_{fix}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{BE} = \frac{C_{fix}}{p - C_{var}}}$$

If the vessel catches **more than** $Q_{BE}$, it makes a profit.

**Condition for profitability:**

$$p > C_{var}$$

If market price falls below variable cost, the vessel should **stop fishing** to minimize losses.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Indicator | When Higher is Better? | Sensitivity to Catch | Sensitivity to Price |
|---|---|---|---|
| Payback Period | No (lower better) | High | Low |
| NPV | Yes | Medium | High |
| ROI | Yes | High | High |
| $C_{pt}$ (Cost/tonne) | No | High | Low |
| BEP Quantity | No | High | High |
| Economic Efficiency | Yes | Medium | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state the **decision rule** after computing any economic indicator. Marks are awarded for **interpretation**, not just arithmetic.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Economic Analysis Framework

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow of economic evaluation** of a fishing vessel, from initial cost inputs to final profitability decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["INPUT: Vessel Specifications"] --> nodeB["Estimate Capital Cost C_cap"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Estimate Annual Operating Cost C_op"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Estimate Annual Catch Q"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Compute Annual Revenue R = Q x p"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Compute Annual Profit P = R minus C_op"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Compute Payback Period T_p"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["Compute NPV over Life L"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["Compute ROI Percentage"]
    nodeF --> nodeJ["Compute Cost per Tonne C_pt"]
    nodeG --> nodeK{"Decision Matrix"}
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK -->|Profitable| nodeL["APPROVE Design"]
    nodeK -->|Marginal| nodeM["OPTIMIZE Design Variables"]
    nodeK -->|Loss| nodeN["REJECT or Redesign"]
    nodeM --> nodeO["Re-iterate: Hull, Engine, Gear"]
    nodeO --> nodeB
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**

- **nodeA → nodeB → nodeC → nodeD → nodeE → nodeF:** The sequential cost-revenue-profit pipeline.
- **nodeF branches into nodeG, nodeH, nodeI, nodeJ:** Four parallel economic indicators are computed from the same profit figure.
- **nodeK is the decision diamond:** All four indicators feed into it for a unified verdict.
- **The feedback loop (nodeM → nodeO → nodeB):** If the design is marginal, the designer tweaks hull form, engine size, or gear type, and **re-runs the entire economic analysis**.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Cost Breakdown Pie Concept

The following diagram represents the **typical proportion of cost components** for a small-to-medium trawler. Values are illustrative averages used in KTU problems.

```mermaid
pie title Typical Cost Distribution for a Medium Trawler
    "Hull and Structure" : 30
    "Main Engine and Propulsion" : 22
    "Fishing Gear and Equipment" : 15
    "Refrigeration and Fish Hold" : 12
    "Electronics and Navigation" : 8
    "Auxiliary Machinery" : 7
    "Outfitting and Accommodation" : 6
```

**Interpretation for students:**

- The **hull** is the single largest cost (~30%) — so hull material choice (steel vs. FRP) has major economic impact.
- The **main engine** is the second largest (~22%) — fuel efficiency directly affects operating cost.
- Together, hull + engine = **~52%** of capital cost. Optimizing these two components gives the biggest economic return.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Operating Cost Flow (Annual Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Vessel in Operation"] --> nodeB["Fuel and Lubricants"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Crew Wages"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Maintenance and Repairs"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["Insurance Premium"]
    nodeA --> nodeF["Port and Harbor Dues"]
    nodeA --> nodeG["Consumables Ice Bait"]
    nodeA --> nodeH["Depreciation Charge"]
    nodeB --> nodeI["Total Annual Operating Cost C_op"]
    nodeC --> nodeI
    nodeD --> nodeI
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Divide by Catch Q"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Cost per Tonne Metric"]
```

**Key Takeaway:** Every node on the left is a **separate recurring expense** that the designer must estimate accurately. Even a 5% reduction in fuel cost can shift a vessel from loss-making to profitable.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define **Payback Period**. State its significance in fishing vessel economics.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Payback Period ($T_p$)** is defined as the time required for the cumulative net profit of a fishing vessel to equal its initial capital investment. It is given by:

$$T_p = \frac{C_{cap}}{P}$$

where $C_{cap}$ is the capital cost and $P$ is the annual net profit.

**Significance:**
1. It indicates how quickly the owner recovers the initial investment — a shorter payback means lower financial risk.
2. It helps compare alternative vessel designs — the design with the shortest payback (within the vessel's economic life) is preferred.
3. If $T_p$ exceeds the vessel's economic life $L$, the investment is uneconomical and the design must be revised.

*(Award: 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for significance.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
List any **six components of annual operating cost** of a fishing vessel.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The six components are:
1. Crew wages and benefits ($C_{crew}$)
2. Fuel and lubricants ($C_{fuel}$)
3. Maintenance and repairs ($C_{maint}$)
4. Insurance premium ($C_{ins}$)
5. Port and harbor dues ($C_{port}$)
6. Depreciation charge ($D$)

*(Award: ½ mark each for any six correctly listed components.)*

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

A 15 m fishing trawler is to be built at a total capital cost of **₹ 1.5 Crore**. The expected annual operating cost is **₹ 35 Lakh**, and the estimated annual catch is **120 tonnes** at an average selling price of **₹ 400 per kg**.

**a) (7 marks)** Calculate the annual net profit, payback period, and cost per tonne of catch. Comment on the economic viability assuming an economic life of **12 years**.

**b) (7 marks)** If the owner can invest in a fuel-efficient engine upgrade costing an additional **₹ 20 Lakh** but reducing annual fuel cost (part of operating cost) by **₹ 5 Lakh**, recalculate the payback period. Should the upgrade be accepted?

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Given Data:**
- $C_{cap} = 1{,}50{,}00{,}000$ (₹ 1.5 Crore)
- $C_{op} = 35{,}00{,}000$ (₹ 35 Lakh)
- $Q = 120$ tonnes
- $p = 400$ per kg $= 4{,}00{,}000$ per tonne
- $L = 12$ years

**Step 1: Annual Revenue**

$$R = Q \times p = 120 \times 4{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}80{,}00{,}000$$

So $R = $ **₹ 4.8 Crore per year**.

**Step 2: Annual Net Profit**

$$P = R - C_{op} = 4{,}80{,}00{,}000 - 35{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}45{,}00{,}000$$

So $P = $ **₹ 4.45 Crore per year**.

**Step 3: Payback Period**

$$T_p = \frac{C_{cap}}{P} = \frac{1{,}50{,}00{,}000}{4{,}45{,}00{,}000} \approx 0.337 \text{ years}$$

Wait — that is unrealistically short. Let us double-check units. The numbers above represent annual profit in rupees. Converting to consistent units:

$$T_p = \frac{1.5 \text{ Crore}}{4.45 \text{ Crore/year}} = 0.337 \text{ years} \approx 4 \text{ months}$$

**Step 4: Cost per Tonne**

$$C_{pt} = \frac{C_{op}}{Q} = \frac{35{,}00{,}000}{120} = 2{,}91{,}667 \text{ per tonne}$$

**Economic Viability Comment:**

Since $T_p = 0.337$ years $\ll L = 12$ years, the vessel recovers its cost in roughly **4 months**, after which it earns **~11.7 years of pure profit**. The design is **highly economically viable**.

*(Award: 1 mark each for R, P, T_p; 1 mark for C_pt; 2 marks for comment.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Step 1: New Capital Cost**

$$C_{cap}^{new} = 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}70{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2: New Operating Cost**

$$C_{op}^{new} = 35{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = 30{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3: New Annual Profit**

$$P_{new} = R - C_{op}^{new} = 4{,}80{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}50{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 4: New Payback Period**

$$T_p^{new} = \frac{C_{cap}^{new}}{P_{new}} = \frac{1{,}70{,}00{,}000}{4{,}50{,}00{,}000} = 0.378 \text{ years}$$

**Step 5: Comparison**

| Parameter | Original | Upgraded |
|---|---|---|
| Capital Cost | ₹ 1.50 Cr | ₹ 1.70 Cr |
| Operating Cost | ₹ 35 Lakh | ₹ 30 Lakh |
| Annual Profit | ₹ 4.45 Cr | ₹ 4.50 Cr |
| Payback | 0.337 yr (~4 mo) | 0.378 yr (~4.5 mo) |

**Decision:** The upgrade increases profit by **₹ 5 Lakh/year** at a marginal increase in payback (~15 days). The upgrade should be **accepted** because:
- Long-term cumulative savings: ₹ 5 Lakh × 12 years = **₹ 60 Lakh**, which is **3 times** the extra investment of ₹ 20 Lakh.
- Reduced fuel cost also improves environmental compliance and engine life.

*(Award: 1 mark for each step; 1 mark for comparison table; 2 marks for decision with justification.)*

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] (Alternative Choice)**

**a) (7 marks)** Explain the **straight-line** and **declining balance** methods of depreciation. A fishing vessel costs **₹ 80 Lakh** with a salvage value of **₹ 8 Lakh** and an economic life of **10 years**. Compute the annual depreciation using the straight-line method.

**b) (7 marks)** Define **Net Present Value (NPV)**. For the vessel above, if the annual profit is **₹ 12 Lakh** and the discount rate is **10%**, compute the NPV and state whether the investment is acceptable.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Explanation of Methods:**

**Straight-Line Depreciation:** An equal amount is deducted from the vessel's value every year. The annual depreciation is constant.

$$D = \frac{C_{cap} - S}{L}$$

**Declining Balance Depreciation:** A fixed percentage of the *remaining* book value is deducted each year. Hence, depreciation is high initially and decreases over time.

$$D_t = C_{cap} \times (1 - r)^{t-1} \times r$$

**Calculation:**

Given:
- $C_{cap} = 80{,}00{,}000$
- $S = 8{,}00{,}000$
- $L = 10$ years

$$D = \frac{80{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000}{10} = \frac{72{,}00{,}000}{10} = 7{,}20{,}000$$

**Annual depreciation = ₹ 7.2 Lakh per year.**

*(Award: 2 marks for method explanation; 1 mark for formula; 2 marks for substitution; 2 marks for answer.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Definition of NPV:**

NPV is the difference between the present value of all future cash inflows (profits) and the initial investment. It accounts for the **time value of money**.

**Formula:**

$$NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{L} \frac{P_t}{(1+i)^t} - C_{cap}$$

**Calculation:**

Given:
- $P = 12{,}00{,}000$ (constant)
- $i = 0.10$
- $L = 10$
- $C_{cap} = 80{,}00{,}000$

**Step 1: Sum the discount factors using geometric series.**

$$\sum_{t=1}^{10} \frac{1}{(1.10)^t} = \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-10}}{0.10}$$

Compute $(1.10)^{10}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.10)^2 &= 1.21 \\
(1.10)^4 &= 1.4641 \\
(1.10)^5 &= 1.61051 \\
(1.10)^{10} &= (1.61051)^2 = 2.59374
\end{aligned}$$

So $(1.10)^{-10} = 1 / 2.59374 = 0.38554$

$$\sum_{t=1}^{10} \frac{1}{(1.10)^t} = \frac{1 - 0.38554}{0.10} = \frac{0.61446}{0.10} = 6.1446$$

**Step 2: Present value of total profits.**

$$PV = 12{,}00{,}000 \times 6.1446 = 73{,}73{,}520$$

**Step 3: NPV.**

$$NPV = 73{,}73{,}520 - 80{,}00{,}000 = -6{,}26{,}480$$

**Decision:** Since $NPV = -₹ 6.27$ Lakh $< 0$, the investment is **NOT acceptable** at 10% discount rate.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition; 2 marks for series sum; 1 mark for PV; 1 mark for NPV; 2 marks for decision.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Students often mix Lakhs and Crores without converting. Always convert to a **single unit** before substituting in formulas. Losing **2 marks** is common here.
> 2. **Forgetting Discounting:** When asked for NPV, students compute simple profit × years without discounting. KTU examiners deduct **up to 4 marks** for this.
> 3. **Wrong Payback Interpretation:** Writing "Payback period = 0.5 years" without stating "≈ 6 months" loses a clarity mark. Always add a **physical interpretation**.
> 4. **Skipping the Decision Rule:** Computing NPV but not stating "accept/reject" leads to loss of **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Confusing Cost and Profit:** Some students subtract capital cost from operating cost instead of revenue. Always remember: $P = R - C_{op}$, not $P = C_{cap} - C_{op}$.
> 6. **Geometric Series Errors:** Forgetting that the NPV sum starts from $t = 1$, not $t = 0$. The capital cost is at $t = 0$ and is **not discounted**.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Economy**
> - [x] **Capital Cost ($C_{cap}$)** = one-time, includes hull, engine, gear, refrigeration, electronics, outfitting.
> - [x] **Operating Cost ($C_{op}$)** = recurring, includes crew wages, fuel, maintenance, insurance, port dues, depreciation.
> - [x] **Revenue ($R$)** = Catch quantity × Market price per unit.
> - [x] **Profit ($P$)** = Revenue − Operating Cost.
> - [x] **Payback Period** = $C_{cap} / P$. Must be less than economic life.
> - [x] **NPV** = Discounted lifetime profit minus capital cost. Positive → accept.
> - [x] **ROI** = (Profit / Capital Cost) × 100%.
> - [x] **Cost per Tonne** = $C_{op} / Q$. Lower is better.
> - [x] **Straight-line depreciation** = $(C_{cap} - S) / L$.
> - [x] **Declining balance** = $C_{cap}(1-r)^{t-1} r$ — high initially, low later.
> - [x] **Break-even catch** = $C_{fix} / (p - C_{var})$.
> - [x] **Economic efficiency** = $\{(R - C_{op}) / R\} \times 100\%$.
> - [x] **Time value of money** must always be considered for long-term investments.
> - [x] **Decision rule** must accompany every indicator — marks are awarded for interpretation.
> - [x] **Fuel economy** is the single biggest controllable operating cost — hull form, engine selection, and speed optimization all matter.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Investment Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Investment Cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Investment Cost** (also termed **Initial Capital Cost**, **Capital Outlay**, or **First Cost**) in the context of fishing vessel economics is defined as the *total expenditure incurred from the conceptual design stage through to the commissioning and outfitting of a fishing vessel, making it operationally ready for sea trials and commercial fishing operations.*

Mathematically, the total investment cost $C_T$ is the summation of all major cost blocks attributable to the new-build project:

$$C_T = C_H + C_M + C_E + C_F + C_O + C_D + C_I$$

where:
- $C_H$ = Hull and structural cost
- $C_M$ = Main propulsion and auxiliary machinery cost
- $C_E$ = Electronic, navigation, and communication equipment cost
- $C_F$ = Fishing gear and deck machinery cost
- $C_O$ = Outfitting, accommodation, and furnishing cost
- $C_D$ = Design, engineering, classification, and consultancy cost
- $C_I$ = Insurance, financing, and project management cost

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The investment cost is a *one-time, sunk cost* incurred at the inception of the project. Unlike operating costs (fuel, crew wages, maintenance), it is **not repeated** during the vessel's economic life, but it directly determines the **fixed cost component**, **depreciation**, and the **capital recovery factor (CRF)** in any discounted cash-flow analysis of a fishing enterprise.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are planning to **open a small restaurant**:
- Before serving the *first plate of food*, you must pay for the building/space, kitchen equipment, furniture, licenses, and decoration.
- These are *one-time, upfront expenses* — you cannot recover them easily, and they are committed even before any income starts flowing.

Similarly, for a fishing vessel:
- Before the boat catches its **very first kilogram of fish**, the owner must already have paid for the **steel hull, engine, winches, nets, GPS, sonar, life-saving equipment, refrigeration plant**, and the architect/naval architect's fees.
- This entire upfront expenditure is the **Investment Cost**.

Think of it as the **"ticket price"** of entering the fishing business — it includes *everything* required to bring the vessel from a blank drawing board to a seaworthy, fish-ready platform.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world metric used by shipbuilders worldwide:** Investment cost is commonly benchmarked against vessel **tonnage (GRT)**, **length overall (LOA)**, or **deadweight (DWT)** to allow comparison between vessels of different sizes. A modern purse-seiner of ~25 m LOA may have an investment cost in the range of **₹ 4 – 8 crore (USD 500,000 – 1,000,000)** depending on fishing systems installed.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Size exponent (cost-vs-size) | $n$ | **0.55 – 0.80** (dimensionless) |
| Steel density | $\rho_{steel}$ | **7850 kg/m³** |
| Hull steel fraction | $f_{steel}$ | **25 – 35 %** of $C_T$ |
| Machinery fraction | $f_{mach}$ | **20 – 30 %** of $C_T$ |
| Electronics fraction | $f_{elec}$ | **5 – 12 %** of $C_T$ |
| Fishing gear fraction | $f_{gear}$ | **8 – 18 %** of $C_T$ |
| Outfitting fraction | $f_{out}$ | **8 – 15 %** of $C_T$ |
| Design/overhead fraction | $f_{des}$ | **5 – 10 %** of $C_T$ |
| Reference cost index | $I_{ref}$ | Year-specific (e.g., base year) |

---

### 1.4 Classification of Investment Cost Components

Investment cost components may be classified along two primary axes used by naval architects:

**Axis A — By Physical Subsystem:**
1. Hull construction (steel plate, fabrication, welding, painting)
2. Propulsion system (main engine, gearbox, propeller, shafting)
3. Auxiliary systems (generators, pumps, compressors, hydraulics)
4. Fishing systems (winches, net haulers, lines, nets, hooks)
5. Fish handling & preservation (refrigeration, ice plant, RSW, freezers)
6. Navigation & electronics (radar, GPS, echo sounder, sonar, VHF, AIS)
7. Safety & life-saving (life rafts, fire-fighting, EPIRBs)
8. Accommodation & galley (cabins, bunks, mess, pantry, HVAC)
9. Documentation & certification (classification, registration, flag fees)

**Axis B — By Cost Type:**
- **Direct Cost:** Material + Labour directly tied to fabrication
- **Indirect Cost:** Design, supervision, insurance, profit margin, overheads
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework

The estimation of investment cost progresses through five sequential logic layers:

#### Layer 1 — Identify the Reference Vessel
- Select a *similar, already-built vessel* whose cost is known (either historical actuals or quoted price).
- Document its principal particulars: $L_{OA}$, $L_{pp}$, $B$, $D$, $T$, $\Delta$, $GRT$, $DWT$, installed power, fishing system type.

#### Layer 2 — Establish Cost Indices
- Locate the **base-year cost index** $I_1$ at the time the reference vessel was built.
- Obtain the **current-year cost index** $I_2$ (e.g., from *Lloyd's Register Shipbuilding Cost Index*, *Norwegian Skipsteknikk*, or regional indices published by Indian shipyards).
- Compute the **escalation multiplier** $k_I = I_2 / I_1$.

#### Layer 3 — Apply the Size-Scaling Law
- The most fundamental rule of marine cost engineering: *cost does NOT scale linearly with size.*
- Use the **power-law relationship** based on dimensional analysis and empirical shipyard data:

$$\frac{C_2}{C_1} = \left(\frac{L_2}{L_1}\right)^n \cdot \frac{I_2}{I_1}$$

- The exponent $n$ accounts for the **economies of scale** — larger vessels gain cost advantage because hull volume (and hence steel weight) grows faster than length.
- For most fishing vessels, **$n \approx 0.6$ to $0.7$**.

#### Layer 4 — Apply Cost Multipliers for Complexity
- A vessel with sophisticated equipment (RSW plant, acoustic instruments) carries a higher per-tonne cost than a simple gill-netter.
- Introduce a **complexity factor** $k_C$ (typically $1.0$ for reference vessel, $1.05$–$1.30$ for upgraded systems).

#### Layer 5 — Sum All Sub-Blocks
- Apply fractional breakdown to obtain a complete cost-build-up and cross-check against parametric estimates.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_T = \sum C_i$ | Total investment cost = sum of all sub-systems | Currency (₹ / USD) |
| 2 | $\dfrac{C_2}{C_1} = \left(\dfrac{L_2}{L_1}\right)^n \cdot \dfrac{I_2}{I_1}$ | Size-and-time scaling law | $n \in [0.55, 0.80]$ |
| 3 | $C = C_{ref} \cdot \left(\dfrac{GT_2}{GT_1}\right)^{0.65} \cdot \dfrac{I_2}{I_1}$ | GRT-based scaling | $GT$ = Gross Tonnage |
| 4 | $C = k \cdot L_{OA}^{\,a} \cdot B^{\,b} \cdot D^{\,c}$ | Multi-variable parametric cost | $k$ = empirical constant |
| 5 | $C_{steel} = W_{steel} \cdot P_{steel} \cdot (1 + f_{fab})$ | Hull steel cost | $W$ in tonnes, $P$ in ₹/t |
| 6 | $W_{steel} = \rho_{steel} \cdot V_{hull} \cdot f_{waste}$ | Steel weight from hull volume | $f_{waste} \approx 1.10$–$1.20$ |
| 7 | $C_{mach} = P_{inst} \cdot p_{mach}$ | Machinery cost vs installed power | $p_{mach}$ in ₹/kW |
| 8 | $C_R = \dfrac{C_T \cdot i \cdot (1+i)^N}{(1+i)^N - 1}$ | Capital Recovery Factor (annualized) | $i$ = interest, $N$ = life (yr) |
| 9 | $D_{annual} = \dfrac{C_T - S_V}{N}$ | Straight-line depreciation per year | $S_V$ = scrap value |
| 10 | $\eta_{scale} = \left(\dfrac{L_2}{L_1}\right)^{n-1}$ | Scale economy ratio (if $n<1$, big is cheaper per unit length) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `n < 1` is critical:** If $n = 1$, cost scales *linearly* with length — every additional metre of vessel costs the same. If $n < 1$, a doubling of length increases cost by less than double — confirming the famous naval architect's maxim: *"a big ship is cheaper per tonne than a small one."*

---

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Shipyard Tendering:** Yard owners use these formulas to produce **indicative quotations** to fishing companies within ±15 % accuracy.
- **Government Subsidy Schemes (India):** The Ministry of Fisheries, Animal Husbandry & Dairying uses parametric cost formulas to determine subsidy ceilings under the *PMMSY (Pradhan Mantri Matsya Sampada Yojana)* scheme.
- **Bank Loan Appraisal:** Financial institutions (NABARD, commercial banks) evaluate the loan-to-asset ratio using these cost estimates before sanctioning vessel-purchase loans.
- **Insurance Valuation:** Marine underwriters use investment cost as the basis for *hull insurance premium* calculation.

---

### 2.4 Sources of Cost Variability

1. **Geographic location of shipyard** (Cochin, Colombo, Guangzhou, or Istanbul — each carries different labour rate).
2. **Series production vs. one-off build** (first vessel in a class is 15–25 % costlier).
3. **Flag state and classification society** (IRS, BV, LR, DNV — each adds different fees).
4. **Exchange rate fluctuations** for imported machinery.
5. **Owner-furnished equipment** vs. yard-furnished equipment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Size-Scaling Law

The classic power-law cost relationship used in marine cost engineering is **empirically derived** from dimensional reasoning combined with statistical regression of historical shipyard data. The logical derivation proceeds as follows.

**Step 1 — Recognize the dominant physical dependency.**

For a geometrically similar vessel (same form coefficients, same proportions), the *hull volume* $V_{hull}$ scales with the cube of any linear dimension $L$:

$$V_{hull} \propto L^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute the hull steel weight.**

Since steel weight $W_{steel}$ equals density $\rho$ times volume times waste factor $f_w$:

$$W_{steel} = \rho_{steel} \cdot V_{hull} \cdot f_{waste} \propto L^3$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that cost is not all material.**

Cost has a *material component* (proportional to weight, hence to $L^3$) and a *labour/overhead component* (proportional to surface area $\propto L^2$ or to length $L^1$). A weighted combination gives:

$$C \propto L^{m}, \quad \text{where } 1 \le m \le 3$$

**Step 4 — Empirical regression fixes the exponent.**

Fitting regression curves to thousands of historical vessel quotations shows $m \approx 1.6$–$2.0$ for most vessel types. When cost is normalized per *unit length*, the exponent collapses to:

$$n = m - 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n \approx 0.6 \text{ to } 0.8$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the final formula.**

Including time escalation via cost index $I$:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{C_2}{C_1} = \left(\dfrac{L_2}{L_1}\right)^n \cdot \dfrac{I_2}{I_1}}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem Statement:**

A 20 m LOA fishing trawler was built in 2015 at a cost of **₹ 1.50 crore**. Estimate the investment cost of a geometrically similar trawler of **25 m LOA** to be built in **2025**, given:

- Size exponent $n = 0.65$
- Cost index in 2015: $I_{2015} = 105$
- Cost index in 2025: $I_{2025} = 168$

**Given Data:**

$$L_1 = 20 \text{ m}, \quad C_1 = ₹ 1.50 \text{ crore}, \quad L_2 = 25 \text{ m}$$
$$n = 0.65, \quad I_1 = 105, \quad I_2 = 168$$

**Step 1 — Compute the length ratio.**

$$\dfrac{L_2}{L_1} = \dfrac{25}{20} = 1.25$$

**Step 2 — Compute the size-scaling factor.**

$$\left(\dfrac{L_2}{L_1}\right)^n = (1.25)^{0.65}$$

Taking logarithm:

$$\ln(1.25)^{0.65} = 0.65 \cdot \ln(1.25) = 0.65 \cdot 0.2231 = 0.1450$$

Therefore:

$$(1.25)^{0.65} = e^{0.1450} \approx 1.1561$$

**Step 3 — Compute the cost-index escalation factor.**

$$\dfrac{I_2}{I_1} = \dfrac{168}{105} = 1.6000$$

**Step 4 — Compute the total cost multiplier.**

$$k_{total} = 1.1561 \times 1.6000 = 1.8498$$

**Step 5 — Compute the new investment cost.**

$$C_2 = C_1 \cdot k_{total} = 1.50 \text{ crore} \times 1.8498$$

$$C_2 \approx 2.775 \text{ crore}$$

**Final Answer:** The estimated investment cost of the 25 m trawler in 2025 is **≈ ₹ 2.77 crore**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cost Breakdown for a Modern 22 m Trawler

| Cost Block | Fraction of $C_T$ | Approx. Value (₹ lakh) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hull steel + fabrication | **30 %** | 75.0 | Includes plate, welding, blasting, painting |
| Main engine + propulsion | **20 %** | 50.0 | Includes gearbox, propeller, shaft |
| Auxiliaries (gen, hydraulics) | **10 %** | 25.0 | 2× generators, pumps, compressors |
| Electronics + navigation | **10 %** | 25.0 | Radar, GPS, sonar, VHF, AIS |
| Fishing gear + deck machinery | **15 %** | 37.5 | Winches, net hauler, trawl gear |
| Refrigeration / fish hold | **5 %** | 12.5 | Plate freezer or RSW |
| Outfitting + accommodation | **5 %** | 12.5 | Cabins, galley, HVAC |
| Design + classification | **5 %** | 12.5 | Naval architect + IRS fees |
| **Total $C_T$** | **100 %** | **₹ 250 lakh (₹ 2.5 cr)** | Indicative only |

---

### 3.4 Verification Using Alternative Parametric Method

**Cross-check using GRT-based formula:**

For a 22 m vessel with $GT \approx 95$:
$$C_2 = C_{ref} \cdot \left(\dfrac{GT_2}{GT_1}\right)^{0.65}$$

Reference: A 20 m vessel with $GT_1 = 70$ cost ₹ 1.50 cr in 2015. Escalated to 2025 first:
$$C_{1,2025} = 1.50 \times \dfrac{168}{105} = 2.40 \text{ cr}$$

Then scaled to 22 m:
$$C_2 = 2.40 \times \left(\dfrac{95}{70}\right)^{0.65} = 2.40 \times (1.357)^{0.65}$$
$$(1.357)^{0.65} = e^{0.65 \cdot \ln(1.357)} = e^{0.65 \cdot 0.3053} = e^{0.1984} \approx 1.2195$$
$$C_2 = 2.40 \times 1.2195 \approx 2.927 \text{ cr}$$

This is consistent with the size-scaling result (≈ 2.77 cr) within typical estimation accuracy (±15 %), confirming the method's reliability.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Investment Cost Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Initiation] --> B[Define Vessel Particulars]
    B --> C[Select Reference Vessel]
    C --> D{Is Cost Data Available?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Obtain Reference Cost C1]
    D -- No --> F[Use Parametric Model]
    E --> G[Identify Cost Indices I1 and I2]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Apply Size Scaling Law n]
    H --> I[Compute Size Factor L2 over L1 power n]
    I --> J[Compute Time Factor I2 over I1]
    J --> K[Multiply All Factors]
    K --> L[Determine Base Cost C2]
    L --> M[Add Complexity Factor kC]
    M --> N[Add Regional Labour Factor kR]
    N --> O[Subdivide into Cost Blocks]
    O --> P[Hull]
    O --> Q[Machinery]
    O --> R[Electronics]
    O --> S[Fishing Gear]
    O --> T[Outfitting]
    O --> U[Design Overheads]
    P --> V[Total Investment Cost CT]
    Q --> V
    R --> V
    S --> V
    T --> V
    U --> V
    V --> W[Cross-Check with Parametric Method]
    W --> X[Final Approved Budget]
```

### 4.2 Cost Composition Pie-Chart (Textual Schematic)

```mermaid
pie title Investment Cost Distribution for 22 m Trawler
    "Hull and Structure" : 30

```

### 4.3 Cost-Build-Up Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CT[Total Investment Cost CT] --> CH[Hull Cost CH]
    CT --> CM[Machinery Cost CM]
    CT --> CE[Electronics Cost CE]
    CT --> CF[Fishing Gear Cost CF]
    CT --> CO[Outfitting Cost CO]
    CT --> CD[Design Cost CD]
    CH --> CH1[Steel Plate]
    CH --> CH2[Fabrication Labour]
    CH --> CH3[Paint and Corrosion Protection]
    CM --> CM1[Main Engine]
    CM --> CM2[Gearbox and Shaft]
    CM --> CM3[Propeller]
    CM --> CM4[Auxiliary Generators]
    CE --> CE1[Radar and GPS]
    CE --> CE2[Sonar and Echo Sounder]
    CE --> CE3[Communication VHF AIS]
    CF --> CF1[Winches and Net Haulers]
    CF --> CF2[Nets Lines Hooks]
    CO --> CO1[Crew Cabins]
    CO --> CO2[Galley and Provision]
    CD --> CD1[Naval Architect Fee]
    CD --> CD2[Classification Fee]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each terminal node (e.g., CH1, CM4) is a *line item* in the shipyard's quotation. When aggregated, they yield the *total investment cost* the owner must finance before the vessel becomes operational.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define the term "Investment Cost" of a fishing vessel. List any four major cost components.*

**Model Answer:**

Investment cost is the **total one-time capital expenditure** required to design, construct, equip, and commission a fishing vessel, making it ready for commercial operation. It is a *sunk cost* incurred at project initiation and forms the basis for calculating depreciation, fixed cost, and loan repayment schedules.

**Four major cost components:**
1. Hull and structural cost ($C_H$) — steel, fabrication, painting.
2. Main propulsion cost ($C_M$) — engine, gearbox, propeller.
3. Fishing gear and deck machinery cost ($C_F$) — winches, nets, lines.
4. Electronics and navigation cost ($C_E$) — radar, GPS, sonar, communication.

*(Any four valid components accepted; award full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State the size-scaling law used in marine cost estimation. Explain the significance of the exponent $n$.*

**Model Answer:**

The size-scaling law is given by:

$$\dfrac{C_2}{C_1} = \left(\dfrac{L_2}{L_1}\right)^n \cdot \dfrac{I_2}{I_1}$$

**Significance of $n$:**
- $n$ is the **size exponent** that quantifies the economies of scale in shipbuilding.
- For fishing vessels, $n$ typically lies in the range **0.55 – 0.80**.
- Since $n < 1$, cost grows *slower* than length — a larger vessel costs less *per metre* than a smaller one.
- This empirical constant is obtained from regression analysis of historical shipyard data.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the major cost components that constitute the investment cost of a fishing vessel. Provide a typical percentage breakdown.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 24 m fishing trawler has an estimated build cost of ₹ 3.20 crore in the year 2023. Using the size-scaling law, estimate the cost of a geometrically similar 30 m trawler to be built in 2026. Take size exponent $n = 0.70$ and cost indices as $I_{2023} = 142$ and $I_{2026} = 175$.

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Cost Components Breakdown:**

| S.No | Component | Description | Typical % |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hull & Structure** | Steel plate, frames, welding, blasting, painting | 25 – 35 % |
| 2 | **Main Propulsion** | Main engine, gearbox, propeller, shafting | 15 – 22 % |
| 3 | **Auxiliary Machinery** | Generators, compressors, pumps, hydraulics | 8 – 12 % |
| 4 | **Fishing Gear & Deck Machinery** | Winches, net haulers, trawl gear, purse seine | 8 – 18 % |
| 5 | **Electronics & Navigation** | Radar, GPS, sonar, VHF, AIS, autopilot | 5 – 12 % |
| 6 | **Refrigeration / Fish Hold** | Ice plant, RSW, plate freezer, insulated hold | 4 – 8 % |
| 7 | **Outfitting & Accommodation** | Cabins, galley, HVAC, sanitation | 8 – 15 % |
| 8 | **Design, Classification & Overheads** | Naval architect, classification society, insurance | 5 – 10 % |

The hull typically dominates because of material weight (≈ 25 % of $C_T$ for steel vessels). Modern trawlers with sophisticated electronics show a higher electronics share (up to 12 %). Owner-furnished items reduce the yard's scope but do not reduce investment cost — only its distribution.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
$$L_1 = 24 \text{ m}, \quad C_1 = 3.20 \text{ crore}, \quad L_2 = 30 \text{ m}$$
$$n = 0.70, \quad I_1 = 142, \quad I_2 = 175$$

**Step 1:** Length ratio:
$$\dfrac{L_2}{L_1} = \dfrac{30}{24} = 1.25$$

**Step 2:** Size factor:
$$(1.25)^{0.70} = e^{0.70 \cdot \ln(1.25)} = e^{0.70 \cdot 0.2231} = e^{0.1562} \approx 1.1690$$

**Step 3:** Time escalation factor:
$$\dfrac{I_2}{I_1} = \dfrac{175}{142} = 1.2324$$

**Step 4:** Combined factor:
$$k_{total} = 1.1690 \times 1.2324 = 1.4406$$

**Step 5:** New investment cost:
$$C_2 = 3.20 \times 1.4406 \approx 4.610 \text{ crore}$$

**Answer:** The estimated cost of the 30 m trawler in 2026 is **₹ 4.61 crore**.

---

**Question B — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various cost estimation methods used in fishing vessel design. Compare their accuracy and applicability.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 28 m purse-seiner cost ₹ 5.50 crore to build in 2020. Compute its estimated replacement cost in 2025, given the cost index rose from $I_{2020} = 125$ to $I_{2025} = 162$. If the owner wants to build a larger 35 m vessel geometrically similar, with size exponent $n = 0.72$, what would be the cost (assuming cost index in build year remains 162)?

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Cost Estimation Methods:**

1. **Detailed (Bottom-Up) Estimation** — Itemizes every nut, bolt, plate, and labour hour. Accuracy ±5 %, but extremely time-consuming; used for final tender-stage quotes.
2. **Comparative (Reference Vessel) Method** — Scales cost of a similar existing vessel using size and time indices. Accuracy ±15 %. Used in *concept and contract design phases*. **Most common in KTU problems.**
3. **Parametric Method** — Uses statistical regression equations: $C = k \cdot L^a \cdot B^b \cdot D^c \cdot (P)^{d}$. Accuracy ±20 %. Used for feasibility studies.
4. **Cost-per-Tonne Methods** — Applies $/GRT or $/DWT multipliers from industry databases. Accuracy ±25 %. Used for preliminary budgetary quotes.
5. **Learning Curve / Series Production Adjustment** — Adjusts cost for the $N$-th vessel in a series using Wright's law. Accuracy ±10 % for series production.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Part (i):** Time-escalated cost of same vessel in 2025:
$$C_{2025} = C_{2020} \cdot \dfrac{I_{2025}}{I_{2020}} = 5.50 \times \dfrac{162}{125}$$
$$C_{2025} = 5.50 \times 1.296 = 7.128 \text{ crore}$$

**Part (ii):** Cost of 35 m vessel in 2025:

Length ratio:
$$\dfrac{L_2}{L_1} = \dfrac{35}{28} = 1.25$$

Size factor:
$$(1.25)^{0.72} = e^{0.72 \cdot 0.2231} = e^{0.1606} \approx 1.1742$$

Now applying both size and time scaling from 2020 base:
$$C_{new} = 5.50 \times (1.25)^{0.72} \times \dfrac{162}{125}$$
$$C_{new} = 5.50 \times 1.1742 \times 1.296$$
$$C_{new} = 5.50 \times 1.5218 \approx 8.370 \text{ crore}$$

**Answer:**
- Replacement cost in 2025 (same 28 m): **₹ 7.13 crore**
- New-build cost of 35 m vessel in 2025: **₹ 8.37 crore**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Index mismatch:** Many students mistakenly use the *same year* index for numerator and denominator — always confirm the base year and target year explicitly.
> 2. **Exponent confusion:** $n$ applies to the length ratio **before** multiplication, NOT to the entire expression. Compute $(L_2/L_1)^n$ *first*, then multiply by $I_2/I_1$.
> 3. **Unit mismatch:** Cost indices must be from the **same source** (don't mix Lloyd's index with regional index). If stated generically, assume consistency.
> 4. **Rounding errors:** Round only the final answer; intermediate steps should retain 4–5 significant figures.
> 5. **Currency unit:** Always state answer with unit (₹ crore / ₹ lakh / USD million).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Investment Cost**

- **Definition:** One-time, upfront capital outlay for new-build fishing vessel — a *sunk cost*.
- **Total cost identity:** $C_T = C_H + C_M + C_E + C_F + C_O + C_D + C_I$.
- **Master formula:** $\dfrac{C_2}{C_1} = \left(\dfrac{L_2}{L_1}\right)^n \cdot \dfrac{I_2}{I_1}$.
- **Size exponent:** $n \in [0.55, 0.80]$ for fishing vessels; typical value **0.65**.
- **Cost index:** Always use same index series; verify base year and current year.
- **Hull dominates:** ~30 % of total cost in steel fishing vessels.
- **Economies of scale:** Because $n < 1$, *larger vessels are cheaper per metre*.
- **GRT-based alternative:** $C \propto (GT)^{0.65}$ — useful when length data unavailable.
- **Capital recovery:** Use $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^N}{(1+i)^N - 1}$ to annualize investment cost.
- **Cross-check:** Always validate the result using a second method (e.g., GRT-based vs length-based).
- **Subsidy relevance:** Indian PMMSY scheme uses these formulas to fix subsidy ceilings.
- **Common exam pitfall:** Don't confuse *investment cost* with *operating cost* — the former is one-time, the latter is recurring.
- **Order of computation:** Length ratio → size factor → index ratio → multiplication → final cost.
- **Significant figures:** Carry 4 digits in intermediate steps; round final to 2 decimal places.
- **Always include units:** ₹ crore / ₹ lakh / USD — never leave the answer unit-less.

---

*End of Notes — Investment Cost | Module 2 | OENST833 — Basics of Fishing Vessel Design*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating Revenues and Costs, Design of Main Dimensions and Form: Parent Vessel Data Analysis
Space Requirement (Capacity) of the Whole Ship
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Space Requirement (Capacity) of the Whole Ship

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Space Requirement (Capacity) of the Whole Ship** is the systematic process of determining the total internal volume, displacement, and weight-carrying capability of a fishing vessel based on its intended operational role, cargo/fish hold needs, propulsion machinery, crew accommodation, fishing gear storage, and consumables. It is the foundational step in **preliminary ship design**, where the naval architect translates the owner's operational requirements into a feasible set of principal dimensions and form coefficients.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this topic is categorized under the **Ship Form Synthesis** phase and directly addresses the question: *"How big must the ship be to do its job?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Space Requirement (Capacity) of the Whole Ship covers displacement calculation, deadweight analysis, block and form coefficients, and the partitioning of internal volume for functional compartments of a fishing vessel.

Mathematically, the total capacity of a ship is expressed in two equivalent ways:

$$
\text{Weight Capacity (Displacement)} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Volume Capacity (Displaced Volume)}
$$

linked by the density of water:

$$
\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla
$$

where $\Delta$ is the displacement (in tonnes or kg), $\nabla$ is the displaced volume (in m³), and $\rho$ is the mass density of water (typically $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \; \text{t/m}^3$ for seawater and $\rho_{fw} = 1.000 \; \text{t/m}^3$ for freshwater).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Designing a Family House**
>
> Imagine an architect is asked to design a house for a family. Before drawing a single wall, the architect must answer: *"How many bedrooms? How big a kitchen? How much storage? Garage for how many cars?"* The **sum of all these needs** defines the *total floor area* of the house, which in turn fixes the length and breadth of the building.
>
> A fishing vessel is no different. The owner specifies: *"I need a 50-tonne fish hold, accommodation for 12 crew, room for a 500 HP engine, 20 tonnes of fuel, and 10 tonnes of fishing gear."* The naval architect adds up all these **weight and volume requirements** to determine the **total displacement** and **principal dimensions** of the vessel.

Think of the ship as a **floating warehouse** that must carry:

1. **The ship itself** (steel hull, machinery, equipment) — called **Lightship Weight**.
2. **Everything the ship must transport to do its job** — called **Deadweight**.

The sum is the **Total Displacement**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Board Exams

The following standard units and constants are routinely used:

- **Displacement ($\Delta$):** in **tonnes (t)** or **metric tonnes**, where 1 t = 1000 kg.
- **Displaced Volume ($\nabla$):** in **cubic metres (m³)**.
- **Density of seawater:** $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \; \text{t/m}^3$.
- **Density of freshwater:** $\rho_{fw} = 1.000 \; \text{t/m}^3$.
- **Principal Dimensions:** Length ($L$), Breadth ($B$), Draft ($T$) — all in **metres (m)**.
- **Form Coefficients:** dimensionless numbers between 0 and 1.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Total Displacement

The total displacement of any ship — including a fishing vessel — is partitioned into two major weight groups:

$$
\Delta_{\text{total}} = W_{\text{Lightship}} + DWT
$$

### 2.1.1 Lightship Weight ($W_{LS}$)

This is the **empty weight of the ship** — the hull structure, machinery, outfit, and fixed equipment. It can be sub-divided as:

$$
W_{LS} = W_{\text{Steel}} + W_{\text{Machinery}} + W_{\text{Outfit}} + W_{\text{Fish Hold Insulation}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The lightship weight is what the ship weighs on the shipyard scale *before* any cargo, fuel, water, or crew is loaded. It is essentially the "empty shell + engine + furniture" weight.

### 2.1.2 Deadweight ($DWT$)

The Deadweight is the **variable load** — everything the ship carries to perform its mission. For a fishing vessel, this is mission-specific and is the heart of the space requirement calculation.

$$
