## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *List any three lifesaving appliances required to be carried on board a fishing vessel of 24 m LOA as per the FAO/ILO/IMO Code of Safety. State one functional requirement for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Appliance | Functional Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Lifejacket (PFD)** | Must provide ≥ **150 N buoyancy** and self-right an unconscious wearer face-up within **5 seconds** (SOLAS LSA Code Ch. II). |
| 2 | **Inflatable Liferaft** | Must inflate automatically via **HRU at ≤ 4 m depth**; must accommodate full crew complement; carry SOLAS A-pack (for ocean-going) with food, water, flares. |
| 3 | **EPIRB (406 MHz)** | Must transmit distress signal for **≥ 48 hours** continuously; float-free activated; registered with national authority. |

> **Examiner Note:** Award 1 mark per correct appliance + functional requirement pair (1×3 = 3 marks).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *What is a net monitoring sonde? Briefly explain its working principle and one typical application.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A net monitoring sonde is a **hydroacoustic sensor** mounted on the trawl headline (or other parts of the gear) that measures parameters such as depth, water temperature, and tilt angle, transmitting the data wirelessly to a surface receiver.
- **Working principle (1 mark):** The sonde contains a **pressure transducer** (for depth, $P = \rho g z$) and an **acoustic transmitter** that emits a high-frequency (**50–200 kHz**) pulse encoded with measured data; the vessel's hull-mounted hydrophone decodes this pulse.
- **Application (1 mark):** Used in **bottom trawling** to keep the headline at a fixed distance off the seabed, preventing damage and improving catch efficiency.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — Lifesaving Appliances

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Q7(a)(b)]**

**(a)** Classify the lifesaving appliances carried on a fishing vessel of 24 m LOA with 18 crew. List, with the governing IMO standard for each:
   (i) Two **personal** appliances.
   (ii) Three **collective** appliances.
   (iii) Two **signaling/locating** appliances.
   **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fishing vessel is to be fitted with inflatable liferafts. Each raft has a capacity of **12 persons**. The vessel carries **18 crew**. Determine:
   (i) The minimum number of liferafts required.
   (ii) The SOLAS side-loading redundancy requirement.
   (iii) The inflation mechanism and HRU activation depth.
   **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Lifesaving Appliances:**

**(i) Personal appliances (2 marks):**
1. **Lifejacket** — SOLAS LSA Code, Chapter II. Buoyancy ≥ 150 N, self-righting < 5 s.
2. **Immersion suit** — SOLAS LSA Code, Chapter III. Maintains body core temperature for ≥ 6 h in 5 °C water.

**(ii) Collective appliances (3 marks):**
1. **Inflatable liferaft** — SOLAS LSA Code, Chapter IV. Inflated by CO₂/N₂; SOLAS A-pack for ocean-going vessels.
2. **Rescue boat** — SOLAS Chapter V. Rigid/fast-recovery craft with outboard motor for MOB retrieval.
3. **Buoyant apparatus** — Torremolinos Protocol. Float-free canister-type with rescue line.

**(iii) Signaling/locating appliances (2 marks):**
1. **EPIRB (406 MHz)** — IMO Resolution A.810(19). Cospas-Sarsat satellite system, ≥ 48 h transmission.
2. **SART** — IMO Resolution A.802(17). 9 GHz X-band radar responder; visible to rescue craft radars up to **8–15 nm**.

---

**(b) Liferaft Sizing Calculation:**

**(i) Minimum number of liferafts (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Apply the governing equation:**

$$
N_{raft} \ge \frac{N_{crew}}{n_{raft}} = \frac{18}{12} = 1.5
$$

**Step 2 — Apply ceiling function:**

$$
N_{raft} = \lceil 1.5 \rceil = 2
$$

**Step 3 — Check the SOLAS side-loading rule:**
Each side of the vessel must accommodate **100 % of the crew** between port and starboard rafts. With two 12-person rafts split port/starboard, each side has **1 × 12 = 12 seats** — which is **less than 18**. Therefore, **re-evaluate**.

**Step 4 — Refine to satisfy redundancy:**
Total raft capacity must equal at least 100 % of the crew: $2 \times 12 = 24 \ge 18$ ✓ — the *total* capacity satisfies SOLAS. For side redundancy on smaller vessels, sometimes an additional raft is required. Under Torremolinos, the **2-raft configuration is acceptable** for this size if HRUs are fitted.

**(ii) SOLAS side-loading redundancy (2 marks):**
The liferafts must be stowed such that the **loss of any one raft does not reduce aggregate capacity below 100 % crew**. Port and starboard stowage is the conventional solution.

**(iii) Inflation mechanism & HRU activation depth (2 marks):**
- **Inflation:** Automatic via **CO₂/N₂ cylinder** triggered by **pulling the painter line** OR automatically via **Hydrostatic Release Unit (HRU)**.
- **HRU activation depth:** **≤ 4 m below waterline** (the HRU's hydrostatic pressure releases a weak link, allowing the raft to float free and inflate).
- **Inflation time:** ≤ **1 minute** at ambient temperature.

---

#### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Net Monitoring

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3, Q8(a)(b)]**

**(a)** What is a net monitoring system? With the aid of a block diagram, describe the architecture of a typical trawl monitoring system. Mention at least **four sensor types** and their functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bottom trawl is being towed at a wire scope of **3.0** in **120 m** of water. The acoustic two-way travel time recorded by the headline depth sensor is **$t = 0.144$ s**. Compute:
   (i) The depth of the headline below the surface.
   (ii) The clearance of the headline above the seabed.
   (iii) Whether the clearance is safe for the gear (assume safe clearance ≥ 1.5 m).
   **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Net Monitoring System:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A net monitoring system is an integrated suite of hydroacoustic, electromechanical, and telemetry sensors fitted on the fishing gear to relay real-time data (depth, height, door spread, symmetry, catch) to the wheelhouse for optimizing gear efficiency.

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.2) — described textually (2 marks):**
Underwater sensors (depth, temperature, doors, symmetry, height, catch) → Acoustic telemetry link (50–200 kHz) → Hull-mounted hydrophone receiver → Signal processor → Wheelhouse LCD display + alarms + data logger → Skipper feedback to towing parameters.

**Four sensor types (3 marks):**
1. **Depth/temperature sonde** — measures headline depth and ambient water temperature (target species identification).
2. **Door sensor (port/starboard)** — measures distance between trawl doors; indicates warp balance.
3. **Symmetry sensor** — measures headline tilt angle; indicates port/stbd asymmetry.
4. **Catch sensor** — measures fill level of codend (load cell or optical sensor) for quota/bycatch management.

---

**(b) Trawl Headline Depth Calculation:**

**Given:**
- Water depth: $D = 120$ m
- Wire scope: $\text{Scope} = 3.0$ (informational — not directly used in depth calculation)
- Two-way acoustic travel time: $t = 0.144$ s
- Speed of sound in seawater: $c = 1500$ m/s

**(i) Headline depth from acoustic travel time (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Use two-way travel equation:**

$$
z = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
z = \frac{1500 \times 0.144}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$
z = \frac{216}{2} = 108 \, \text{m}
$$

> **Headline depth = 108 m below the surface.**

---

**(ii) Clearance above seabed (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Clearance} = D - z = 120 - 108 = 12 \, \text{m}
$$

> **Clearance = 12 m above the seabed.**

---

**(iii) Safety evaluation (2 marks):**
Safe clearance requirement: **≥ 1.5 m**.
Actual clearance: **12 m >> 1.5 m**.

> **Conclusion:** The clearance is **safe but excessive** — the trawl is floating too high above the seabed. The skipper should **pay out more warp** (decrease scope) to bring the headline closer to the bottom (target clearance ~ 1.5–3.0 m for demersal trawls). The gear is currently **not in optimal fishing position**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the acoustic travel equation — students write $z = c \cdot t$ instead of $z = c \cdot t / 2$. This loses **2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Confusing "wire scope" with "depth"** — scope is the ratio of warp length to depth; students often substitute it incorrectly as a depth value.
> 3. **Not converting LOA / draft to meters** when calculating lifejacket/liferaft capacity — KTU expects SI units throughout.
> 4. **Omitting SOLAS redundancy check** in liferaft problems — examiners explicitly look for the "each side accommodates 100 % crew" verification.
> 5. **Using fresh-water density (1000 kg/m³) instead of seawater (1025 kg/m³)** in buoyancy problems — a classic 1-mark trap.
> 6. **Skipping units** in final answers — marks are deducted even for correct numerical values if units (m, s, N, Hz, kg) are missing.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

✅ **Lifesaving — Key Takeaways:**
- **SOLAS LSA Code** + **Torremolinos Protocol** govern fishing vessel lifesaving.
- Three-tier hierarchy: **Personal → Collective → Signaling**.
- **Lifejacket buoyancy ≥ 150 N**, self-righting **< 5 s**.
- **EPIRB = 406 MHz, ≥ 48 h, Cospas-Sarsat**.
- **SART = 9 GHz X-band, range 8–15 nm**.
- Liferaft HRU activates at **≤ 4 m depth**.
- Liferaft inflation time: **≤ 1 minute**.
- Lifejacket drop test factor: **≥ 2.0× design load**.
- SOLAS side-loading: each side must accommodate **100 % crew**.

✅ **Net Monitoring — Key Takeaways:**
- Acoustic telemetry link: **50–200 kHz**.
- Sound speed in seawater: **c = 1500 m/s**.
- Depth formula: $z = c \cdot t / 2$.
- Doorspread formula: $D = c \cdot t_{door} / 2$.
- Sensor types: **Depth, Temperature, Door, Symmetry, Height, Catch, Bottom Contact**.
- Wheelhouse display units: **Scanmar, Marport, Simrad ITI**.
- Net monitoring **saves fuel (~20–30 %)** and **reduces gear loss**.
- Wire scope: $L_{wire} / D = 2.5 \text{–} 4.0$ typical.
- Tilt angle (symmetry): $\theta = \arctan(\Delta h / L_{head})$.

✅ **Cross-Cutting Points to Memorize:**
- Sound in **seawater** vs **fresh water** (1500 vs 1435 m/s).
- **HRU = 4 m**, **Lifejacket self-right = 5 s**, **EPIRB = 48 h / 406 MHz**.
- Always state **units** in final KTU answers.
- Standard **cospas-sarsat satellite** system for EPIRB detection.
- **SOLAS A-pack vs B-pack** liferaft contents (A: ocean-going, 24-h survival; B: coastal).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy:** In KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3 questions frequently combine a **3-mark definition question** on lifesaving appliances with a **14-mark numerical problem** on net monitoring depth/door-spread calculations. Practice deriving the **two-way acoustic travel equation** with multiple time-velocity scenarios — this is the highest-weightage sub-topic in this module.

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of fishing vessel design, **mechanical properties of materials** refer to the set of measurable characteristics that describe a material's response and behavior when subjected to applied forces, deformations, loads, or environmental conditions during the construction, launch, service life, and eventual scrapping of a vessel. These properties govern the ability of hull plating, frames, girders, bulkheads, deck plating, and superstructure members to withstand the complex multi-axial loading imposed by hydrostatic pressure, wave slamming, slamming loads, bending moments, torsional moments, cargo, machinery vibration, and thermal gradients.

Per the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) for Ocean Engineering and Naval Architecture, mechanical properties form the bedrock for material selection in shipbuilding (Rule 1 of any Classification Society — Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, IRS). They are categorized into:

1. **Elastic Properties** — Stress-strain response in the reversible deformation regime.
2. **Plastic Properties** — Stress-strain response in the irreversible deformation regime.
3. **Strength Properties** — Yield, Ultimate, and Fracture limits.
4. **Energy Properties** — Toughness, Resilience, and Modulus of Resilience.
5. **Surface/Hardness Properties** — Resistance to indentation, abrasion, wear.
6. **Time/Temperature-Dependent Properties** — Fatigue, Creep, Stress Relaxation.
7. **Special Marine Properties** — Corrosion resistance, Galvanic compatibility, Weldability, Notch toughness (Charpy V-notch impact at low temperature).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A fishing vessel engineer must NOT confuse *design of marine structures* with *material selection*. The hull shape, scantlings (plate thickness, frame spacing), and connections are designed to keep *internal stresses* BELOW allowable limits dictated by the *mechanical properties* of the chosen material.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are designing a **traditional catamaran fishing canoe** used by Kerala's coastal fishermen. You have two candidate materials:

- **Material A:** A high-strength quenched-tempered mild steel plate.
- **Material B:** A marine-grade aluminum alloy (5083-H116) plate.

Both must resist the same bending from waves, but they feel completely different to the designer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The "Rubber Band vs. Steel Wire" Intuition:**
>
> Think of a fishing vessel's hull girder as a giant **elastic backbone**. If you pull on a rubber band (low stiffness), it stretches a lot — that is **high strain, low Young's modulus**. If you pull on a steel wire (high stiffness), it barely stretches — that is **low strain, high Young's modulus**. Now imagine pushing the rubber band past its stretchy limit — it deforms permanently and never returns to original shape — that is the **plastic region**. The point where this happens is the **yield point**.
>
> In a fishing vessel:
> - **Hull plates** act like the steel wire — they must NOT stretch too much under wave load.
> - **Fendering (rubber, HDPE bumpers)** acts like the rubber band — they MUST deform to absorb the impact of a berthing trawler.
> - The choice between "rubber band" and "steel wire" behaviour is governed by **mechanical properties**.

### 1.3 Why Mechanical Properties are Pivotal in Fishing Vessel Design

Fishing vessels are unique engineering artifacts because they operate in a **highly corrosive, dynamically loaded, partially submerged environment**. The mechanical properties must therefore satisfy a triplet of constraints simultaneously:

| Constraint | Imposed Demand | Relevant Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Static Strength** | Hydrostatic pressure, still-water bending moment | Yield Strength ($f_y$), Ultimate Tensile Strength ($f_u$) |
| **Dynamic Strength** | Wave-induced slamming, whipping, vibration | Fatigue Limit ($\sigma_f$), Notch Toughness (Charpy $KV$) |
| **Environmental Durability** | Salt water, biofouling, low temperature | Corrosion rate (mm/year), Ductility (% elongation) |

### 1.4 Standard Reference Codes & Metrics

The following internationally recognized standards govern the numerical values for mechanical properties in shipbuilding:

- **ISO 630** — Structural steels for shipbuilding (Grades A, B, D, E).
- **ASTM A131** — Standard Specification for Structural Steel for Ships.
- **EN 10025** — Hot rolled products of structural steels (Grades S235, S275, S355).
- **ASTM B209 / B928** — Aluminum and aluminum alloys for marine applications.
- **ISO 12215** — Small craft hull construction and scantlings (relevant for fishing vessels < 24 m).
- **GL / DNV / ABS Rules** — Classification society rules for fishing vessels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Metric:** The minimum design **yield strength** for mild shipbuilding steel is **235 MPa** (Grade A), while high-tensile shipbuilding steel (AH36/DH36/EH36) has a minimum yield strength of **355 MPa**.

### 1.5 Material Categories Used in Fishing Vessels

| Material Family | Specific Grade | Typical Fishing Vessel Application |
|---|---|---|
| Mild Carbon Steel | Grade A/B ship steel (ASTM A131) | Hull plating, frames, girders of steel trawlers |
| High Tensile Steel | AH36, DH36, EH36 | Longitudinals, sheer strakes, hatch coamings |
| Aluminum Alloy | 5083-H116, 6082-T6 | Hulls of FRP boats, deckhouses, superstructures |
| Fiber Reinforced Plastic (FRP) | Isophthalic polyester + chopped/woven roving | Hulls of canoes, catamarans, small mechanized boats |
| Marine Timber | Teak, Sal, Irul (Kerala species) | Plank-on-frame traditional boats (Vallam, Dhoni) |
| Stainless Steel | AISI 316L | Rudder shafts, fasteners, fishing equipment |
| Copper-Nickel (Cu-Ni) | 90/10, 70/30 | Sea-water piping systems |
| Rubber / HDPE | UHMW-PE | Fenders, bearing pads |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Concept: Stress and Strain

When an external force ($F$) is applied to a body of cross-sectional area ($A$), an **internal resistive force per unit area** is generated. This intensity is called **stress ($\sigma$)**.

The corresponding **deformation per unit original length** is called **strain ($\epsilon$)**.

For a uniaxial loading scenario on a fishing vessel structural member:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

$$\epsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

where:
- $F$ = applied axial force (in **N** or **kN**)
- $A_0$ = original cross-sectional area (in **mm²** or **m²**)
- $\Delta L$ = change in length (in **mm** or **m**)
- $L_0$ = original gauge length (in **mm** or **m**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering vs. True Stress:** For KTU exam purposes, you will use **engineering stress** (using $A_0$). True stress uses the instantaneous (reduced) area $A_i$ in the necking region and becomes relevant in deep plastic analysis.

### 2.2 Types of Mechanical Stress in a Fishing Vessel Hull

A fishing vessel experiences **multi-axial stress** even under simple wave loading. The principal types are:

1. **Normal Stress ($\sigma$)** — Acts perpendicular to a cross-section.
   - **Tensile stress ($\sigma_t$):** Positive, member in tension (e.g., keel plate in sagging).
   - **Compressive stress ($\sigma_c$):** Negative, member in compression (e.g., keel plate in hogging).
2. **Shear Stress ($\tau$)** — Acts parallel to a cross-section (e.g., riveted/bolted plate connections).
3. **Bearing Stress ($\sigma_b$)** — Acts locally under a bolt head or pin.
4. **Hygroscopic / Thermal Stress ($\sigma_{th}$)** — From restrained thermal expansion or moisture-induced swelling (critical in wooden boats).

### 2.3 Hooke's Law and the Three Elastic Moduli

In the **elastic region** (stress below proportional limit), strain is linearly proportional to stress. The constant of proportionality is the **Modulus of Elasticity** or **Young's Modulus ($E$)**.

$$\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon$$

This linear-elastic regime is the foundation of **linear ship structural analysis** and **finite element analysis (FEA)** for fishing vessels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Engineering Elastic Moduli (Must Memorize):**
> - **Young's Modulus ($E$)** — Resistance to uniaxial tension/compression (longitudinal stiffness).
> - **Shear Modulus ($G$)** — Resistance to shear deformation (torsional rigidity of hull).
> - **Bulk Modulus ($K$)** — Resistance to uniform volumetric compression (cavitation analysis).

For an **isotropic** material (e.g., mild steel, aluminum), only two of these are independent:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)}$$

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$

where $\nu$ is the **Poisson's ratio** (ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain under axial loading):

$$\nu = -\frac{\epsilon_{lateral}}{\epsilon_{longitudinal}}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is your **high-yield reference table** for the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE and Module Internal examinations. Print this on a single index card.

| # | Property | Formula | SI Units | Typical Value (Mild Steel) | Marine Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Normal Stress | $\sigma = F / A_0$ | Pa (N/m²) | $\leq 235$ MPa (yield) | Hull plating tension/compression |
| 2 | Shear Stress | $\tau = V / A$ | Pa | $\leq 0.6 f_y$ | Riveted/bolted joint shear |
| 3 | Bearing Stress | $\sigma_b = F_b / (d \cdot t)$ | Pa | $\leq 1.5 f_y$ | Bolt-to-plate contact |
| 4 | Engineering Strain | $\epsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | dimensionless | 0.001 to 0.30 | All structural members |
| 5 | Young's Modulus | $E = \sigma / \epsilon$ | Pa | **200 GPa** | Hull girder stiffness |
| 6 | Shear Modulus | $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ | Pa | **80 GPa** | Torsional rigidity |
| 7 | Bulk Modulus | $K = E / [3(1-2\nu)]$ | Pa | **160 GPa** | Pressure hulls, deep submergence |
| 8 | Poisson's Ratio | $\nu = -\epsilon_{lat}/\epsilon_{long}$ | dimensionless | **0.30** | Lateral contraction under tension |
| 9 | Yield Strength | $f_y$ (from test) | Pa | **235 MPa** (Grade A) | Allowable stress for LR/ABS |
| 10 | Ultimate Tensile Strength | $f_u$ (from test) | Pa | **400–490 MPa** | Reserve strength factor |
| 11 | Modulus of Resilience | $U_r = \sigma_y^2 / (2E)$ | J/m³ | **0.138 MJ/m³** | Energy absorbed elastically |
| 12 | Modulus of Toughness | $U_t = \int \sigma \, d\epsilon$ | J/m³ | **~200 MJ/m³** | Total energy to fracture |
| 13 | Ductility (% Elongation) | $\%EL = (L_f - L_0)/L_0 \times 100$ | % | **22% (min)** | Formability, warning before fracture |
| 14 | Area Reduction | $\%RA = (A_0 - A_f)/A_0 \times 100$ | % | **40% (min)** | Necking severity |
| 15 | Fatigue Limit (Steel) | $\sigma_f \approx 0.5 f_u$ | Pa | **~210 MPa** | Cyclic wave loading |
| 16 | Endurance Ratio (Al) | $\sigma_f / f_u \approx 0.30$ | — | — | Al has NO true fatigue limit |
| 17 | Charpy Impact Energy | $KV$ at design $T$ | J | **27 J @ 0°C** | Notch toughness for low-temp service |
| 18 | Hardness (Brinell) | $HB = F / (\pi D/2)(D - \sqrt{D^2-d^2})$ | — | **120–180 HB** | Wear resistance |
| 19 | Thermal Stress | $\sigma_{th} = E \alpha \Delta T$ | Pa | — | Welded plates, sun-heated deck |
| 20 | Creep Rate (Steady) | $\dot{\epsilon} = A\sigma^n \exp(-Q/RT)$ | s⁻¹ | — | Engine exhaust, steam pipes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Table Mistake to Avoid:** Do NOT confuse **Modulus of Resilience** ($U_r = \sigma_y^2/2E$) with **Modulus of Toughness** ($U_t = \int \sigma d\epsilon$). Resilience is the area UNDER the elastic portion of the stress-strain curve; Toughness is the area UNDER the ENTIRE curve up to fracture.

### 2.5 Detailed Mechanics of Each Property

#### 2.5.1 Elastic Deformation (Reversible)

The material returns to its original shape after load removal. Governed by $E$, $G$, $\nu$. For fishing vessels, the hull is designed to remain in this regime under normal service loads (Working Stress Design approach).

**Hooke's Law in 3D (Generalized form):**

$$\epsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\epsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\epsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}, \quad \gamma_{yz} = \frac{\tau_{yz}}{G}, \quad \gamma_{zx} = \frac{\tau_{zx}}{G}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For exam:** You may be asked to derive lateral strain given longitudinal strain and Poisson's ratio. The relationship is $\epsilon_{lat} = -\nu \cdot \epsilon_{long}$.

#### 2.5.2 Plastic Deformation (Irreversible)

When stress exceeds the **yield strength ($f_y$)**, the material deforms permanently. Atomic planes slide past each other via **dislocation motion**. The strain hardening modulus ($E_{sh}$) governs subsequent stress increase:

$$E_{sh} = \frac{d\sigma}{d\epsilon} \quad \text{in plastic region}$$

Plastic deformation in fishing vessels occurs during:
- Cold forming of plates (roll-bending, line-heating).
- Accidental overload (grounding, berthing impact).
- Extreme storm conditions.

#### 2.5.3 True Stress–True Strain (For Deep Ductility Analysis)

After necking begins:

$$\sigma_T = \sigma(1 + \epsilon)$$

$$\epsilon_T = \ln(1 + \epsilon)$$

where $\sigma_T$ = true stress, $\epsilon_T$ = true strain, $\sigma$ = engineering stress, $\epsilon$ = engineering strain.

#### 2.5.4 Failure Theories (For Multi-Axial Stress States)

A fishing vessel member rarely experiences pure uniaxial stress. For ductile marine steels, the applicable failure theories are:

**Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca):**

$$\sigma_{eq,Tresca} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \leq f_y$$

**Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises):**

$$\sigma_{eq,VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}{2}} \leq f_y$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For ship classification society rules, the **von Mises** yield criterion is most commonly used, especially in FEA-based scantling calculations per ISO 12215-5.

#### 2.5.5 Fatigue

Fishing vessels experience **billions of wave-loading cycles** during their 20–30 year service life. Fatigue failure occurs at stress levels BELOW $f_y$ if the cyclic range is high enough.

**S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve):** Stress amplitude ($S$) vs. Number of cycles to failure ($N$).

For ferrous alloys (steel):
$$S^a \cdot N = C$$

For aluminum (no true endurance limit):
$$S = \sigma'_f (2N)^b$$

where $\sigma'_f$ = fatigue strength coefficient, $b$ = fatigue strength exponent (typically $-0.10$ to $-0.12$).

**Goodman's Modified Equation for Marine Steels:**

$$\sigma_a = \sigma'_f \left[1 - \left(\frac{\sigma_m}{f_u}\right)\right]$$

where $\sigma_a$ = allowable alternating stress, $\sigma_m$ = mean stress.

**Miner's Rule for Cumulative Damage:**

$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i} \leq 1$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** If $D < 1$, no failure predicted. If $D \geq 1$, failure predicted. Students often forget to multiply by partial cycles or to convert $S$ to stress amplitude properly.

#### 2.5.6 Creep

At elevated temperatures ($T > 0.4 T_m$, where $T_m$ = melting point in Kelvin), materials deform slowly under sustained load. Critical for:
- Boiler tubes (steam propulsion).
- Engine exhaust manifolds.
- Fire-fighting engine components.

Three stages: **Primary (decelerating), Secondary (steady-state), Tertiary (accelerating to rupture)**.

**Steady-state creep rate (Power-law creep):**

$$\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} = A \sigma^n \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

where $A, n$ = material constants, $Q$ = activation energy, $R$ = universal gas constant, $T$ = absolute temperature.

#### 2.5.7 Hardness

Measures resistance to localized plastic deformation. Three common scales in marine industry:

- **Brinell Hardness (HB):** Indenter = 10 mm tungsten carbide ball, $F = 3000$ kgf. Used for castings and forgings.
- **Vickers Hardness (HV):** Square diamond pyramid, versatile for small areas (welds, HAZ).
- **Rockwell Hardness (HRB/HRC):** Diamond cone or steel ball, quick shop-floor measurement.

Approximate **empirical conversions** (valid only for steel, KTU may ask this):

$$f_u (\text{MPa}) \approx 3.45 \times HB$$

$$\sigma_y (\text{MPa}) \approx 0.32 \times HB \quad (\text{for mild steel})$$

#### 2.5.8 Notch Toughness and Charpy V-Notch Test

Fishing vessels operating in cold seas (e.g., Arabian Sea winter, North Atlantic fishing) require the hull steel to resist **brittle fracture** at low temperatures.

The **Charpy V-Notch Impact Test** measures the energy absorbed ($KV$ in Joules) by a standardized notched specimen broken by a swinging pendulum.

**Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT):** The temperature at which $KV = 20$ J (typical acceptance criterion for ship steel).

For Grade A ship steel: $KV \geq 27$ J at $+20$°C.
For Grade D ship steel: $KV \geq 27$ J at $-20$°C.
For Grade E ship steel: $KV \geq 27$ J at $-40$°C.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for fishing vessels:** A sudden hull failure in cold seas (e.g., loss of a 12 m fishing trawler off Kochi coast in December) can be catastrophic. The Charpy test ensures the steel remains ductile at expected service temperatures.

#### 2.5.9 Specific Material Mechanical Properties for Fishing Vessels

| Material | $E$ (GPa) | $\nu$ | $f_y$ (MPa) | $f_u$ (MPa) | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | $f_y / \rho$ (kN·m/kg) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel (Grade A) | 200 | 0.30 | 235 | 400–490 | 7850 | 29.9 | Hull plating |
| HT Steel (AH36) | 200 | 0.30 | 355 | 490–620 | 7850 | 45.2 | Longitudinals |
| Aluminum 5083-H116 | 70 | 0.33 | 215 | 305 | 2660 | 80.8 | Light craft hulls |
| Aluminum 6082-T6 | 70 | 0.33 | 250 | 290 | 2700 | 92.6 | Superstructures |
| FRP (Polyester + Glass) | 7–15 | 0.30 | 100–200 | 150–300 | 1500–1800 | 66–133 | Canoes, small boats |
| Teak Wood | 10–13 | — | 50–80 (compression) | 80–120 | 650–800 | 75–125 | Traditional boats |
| Concrete (Reinforced) | 30 | 0.20 | 30 (concrete) | — | 2400 | 12.5 | Pontoons, floats |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Although FRP has lower absolute $f_y$ than steel, its **specific strength** ($f_y/\rho$) is often 2-4× higher, which is why modern fishing catamarans are predominantly FRP.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship between $E$, $G$, and $\nu$

Consider a **pure shear test** on a small cubic element of an isotropic marine steel.

The shear stress $\tau$ produces a shear strain $\gamma$. We can geometrically decompose this shear into two perpendicular normal strains along the diagonals.

**Step 1:** When shear stress $\tau$ is applied to the faces of the cube, the diagonals experience pure tension and compression.

$$\sigma_{tension, diagonal} = +\tau, \quad \sigma_{compression, diagonal} = -\tau$$

**Step 2:** Apply Hooke's Law in 2D to find the strains along the diagonals (45° rotated axes):

$$\epsilon_{tension} = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_{tension} - \nu \sigma_{compression}\right]$$

Substituting the diagonal stresses:

$$\epsilon_{tension} = \frac{1}{E}\left[\tau - \nu(-\tau)\right] = \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

$$\epsilon_{compression} = \frac{1}{E}\left[-\tau - \nu(\tau)\right] = -\frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 3:** Relate the shear strain $\gamma$ to the normal strains along the diagonals. By geometry of deformation:

The shear strain equals the change in the right angle, which is twice the engineering normal strain along the diagonal:

$$\gamma = \epsilon_{tension} - \epsilon_{compression} = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E} - \left(-\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}\right)$$

$$\gamma = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that by definition, the shear modulus is $G = \tau / \gamma$:

$$\gamma = \frac{\tau}{G}$$

**Step 5:** Equate the two expressions for $\gamma$:

$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 6:** Cancel $\tau$ from both sides and solve for $G$:

$$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}}$$

**Numerical Check for Mild Steel:** $E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.30$.

$$G = \frac{200}{2(1 + 0.30)} = \frac{200}{2.60} = 76.92 \text{ GPa}$$

This matches the standard tabulated value of $G \approx 80$ GPa for mild steel. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Bulk Modulus from Hydrostatic Pressure Test

**Setup:** A small cubic specimen of side $a$ is subjected to uniform hydrostatic pressure $p$ from all six faces.

**Step 1:** On each face, the compressive normal stress is $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$.

**Step 2:** The volumetric strain is defined as:

$$\epsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V_0}$$

For small strains, the volume change of a cube is:

$$\Delta V = V_0(\epsilon_x + \epsilon_y + \epsilon_z)$$

So:

$$\epsilon_v = \epsilon_x + \epsilon_y + \epsilon_z$$

**Step 3:** Apply 3D Hooke's Law to find each normal strain:

$$\epsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[-p - \nu(-p + (-p))\right]$$

$$\epsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[-p + 2\nu p\right] = \frac{-p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

By symmetry: $\epsilon_x = \epsilon_y = \epsilon_z = \dfrac{-p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$.

**Step 4:** Sum the three strains:

$$\epsilon_v = 3 \times \frac{-p(1 - 2\nu)}{E} = \frac{-3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 5:** By definition, the bulk modulus is the negative ratio of pressure change to volumetric strain:

$$K = -\frac{p}{\epsilon_v} = -\frac{p}{\frac{-3p(1-2\nu)}{E}} = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$$

$$\boxed{K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}}$$

**Numerical Check for Mild Steel:** $E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.30$.

$$K = \frac{200}{3(1 - 0.60)} = \frac{200}{1.20} = 166.67 \text{ GPa}$$

This matches the standard tabulated value of $K \approx 160$ GPa. ✓

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Modulus of Resilience from Stress-Strain Curve

**Setup:** Consider the elastic portion of a uniaxial tensile test of a marine steel specimen.

**Step 1:** Strain energy density (energy per unit volume) absorbed by the specimen when loaded elastically to stress $\sigma$ is:

$$U = \int_0^{\epsilon} \sigma \, d\epsilon$$

**Step 2:** In the elastic region, $\sigma = E\epsilon$, so $d\sigma = E \, d\epsilon$, or $d\epsilon = d\sigma / E$.

$$U = \int_0^{\sigma} \sigma \cdot \frac{d\sigma}{E} = \frac{1}{E}\int_0^{\sigma} \sigma \, d\sigma$$

**Step 3:** Integrate:

$$U = \frac{1}{E} \cdot \frac{\sigma^2}{2}\bigg|_0^{\sigma} = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E}$$

**Step 4:** For maximum elastic energy storage, set $\sigma = \sigma_y$ (yield stress). This defines the **Modulus of Resilience ($U_r$)**:

$$\boxed{U_r = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}}$$

**Numerical Example:** For a fishing vessel's mild steel hull plate with $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa and $E = 200$ GPa:

$$U_r = \frac{(235 \times 10^6)^2}{2 \times (200 \times 10^9)} = \frac{5.5225 \times 10^{16}}{4 \times 10^{11}}$$

$$U_r = 1.3806 \times 10^5 \text{ J/m}^3 = 0.138 \text{ MJ/m}^3$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Thermal Stress in a Constrained Hull Plate

**Setup:** A steel hull plate of length $L_0$ is constrained between two rigid bulkheads. The plate experiences a temperature rise $\Delta T$.

**Step 1:** If unconstrained, the free thermal expansion would be:

$$\Delta L_{free} = \alpha L_0 \Delta T$$

where $\alpha$ = coefficient of linear thermal expansion. For steel, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$.

**Step 2:** Because the bulkheads prevent this expansion, a compressive force $F$ develops in the plate such that the mechanically-induced contraction equals the free thermal expansion:

$$\Delta L_{mech} = \frac{F L_0}{A E} = \alpha L_0 \Delta T$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $F$:

$$F = A E \alpha \Delta T$$

**Step 4:** The corresponding thermal stress is:

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{F}{A} = E \alpha \Delta T$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{th} = E \alpha \Delta T}$$

**Numerical Example:** Steel plate, $E = 200$ GPa, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$, $\Delta T = 50$°C (sun-heated deck on a Kerala summer noon):

$$\sigma_{th} = (200 \times 10^9)(12 \times 10^{-6})(50)$$

$$\sigma_{th} = 1.2 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = 120 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Design Note:** If this 120 MPa exceeds the allowable stress (typically $0.6 f_y = 141$ MPa for Grade A), the plate will yield or buckle. Fishing vessels therefore use **expansion joints** in long superstructures and **sliding bearings** for hot pipes.

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Deformation under Self-Weight (Hull Girder Sagging)

**Setup:** A simply-supported hull girder (fishing trawler) of length $L$, weight $W$ (including cargo, machinery, fish catch), with mid-span deflection $\delta$.

**Step 4a:** For uniform loading $w = W/L$ on a simply-supported beam:

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{5 w L^4}{384 EI} = \frac{5 W L^3}{384 EI}$$

**Step 4b:** Maximum bending moment at midspan:

$$M_{max} = \frac{W L}{8}$$

**Step 4c:** Maximum bending stress in the keel plate (extreme fiber, distance $c$ from neutral axis):

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max} c}{I} = \frac{W L c}{8 I}$$

**Numerical Example:** Consider a 20 m fishing trawler with $L = 20$ m, $W = 1500$ kN, hull depth $H = 2.5$ m, $c = 1.25$ m, moment of inertia $I = 0.5$ m⁴, $E = 200$ GPa.

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{(1500 \times 10^3)(20)(1.25)}{8 \times 0.5} = 9.375 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 9.375 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{5 (1500 \times 10^3) (20)^3}{384 \times (200 \times 10^9) \times 0.5}$$

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{6 \times 10^{10}}{3.84 \times 10^{13}} = 1.5625 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.56 \text{ mm}$$

This is well within elastic limits and within the typical allowable deflection of $L/500 = 40$ mm. ✓

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Stress-Strain Regions

| Region | Stress Range | Strain Range | Deformation Type | Atomic Mechanism | Ship Design Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Elastic – Linear | $0$ to $\sigma_p$ | $0$ to $\epsilon_p$ | Fully reversible | Bond stretching | Normal service — OK |
| 2. Elastic – Non-linear | $\sigma_p$ to $\sigma_y$ | $\epsilon_p$ to $\epsilon_y$ | Reversible, non-linear | Bond stretching + micro-slip | Approaching yield — warning |
| 3. Plastic – Strain Hardening | $\sigma_y$ to $\sigma_u$ | $\epsilon_y$ to $\epsilon_u$ | Permanent, hardening | Dislocation multiplication | Cold forming — OK |
| 4. Necking | $\sigma_u$ (peak) | Localized | Triaxial stress state | Micro-void coalescence | Approaching fracture — DANGER |
| 5. Fracture | $f_u$ (drops) | $\epsilon_f$ | Separation | Cleavage / ductile rupture | FAILURE |

where:
- $\sigma_p$ = proportional limit
- $\sigma_y$ = yield strength
- $\sigma_u$ = ultimate tensile strength
- $\epsilon_y, \epsilon_u, \epsilon_f$ = corresponding strains

### 3.7 Worked Numerical Problem

**Problem:** A 12 m fishing trawler's keel plate (Grade A mild steel, $f_y = 235$ MPa, $f_u = 400$ MPa, $E = 200$ GPa) has a cross-section of 300 mm × 12 mm. During a particularly severe wave, the keel experiences an axial tensile force of 800 kN. Determine:

(a) The engineering stress.
(b) The engineering strain.
(c) Whether the keel remains in the elastic region.
(d) The lateral strain, assuming Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.30$.

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Engineering Stress**

Cross-sectional area:

$$A_0 = 300 \times 12 = 3600 \text{ mm}^2 = 3.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Engineering stress:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{800 \times 10^3}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.222 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = 222.22 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part (b): Engineering Strain**

Using Hooke's Law (assume elastic, to be verified in (c)):

$$\epsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{222.22 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 1.111 \times 10^{-3} = 0.1111\%$$

**Part (c): Elasticity Check**

Yield strain:

$$\epsilon_y = \frac{f_y}{E} = \frac{235 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 1.175 \times 10^{-3}$$

Since $\epsilon = 1.111 \times 10^{-3} < \epsilon_y = 1.175 \times 10^{-3}$, the keel remains **elastic** with a safety factor:

$$\text{SF} = \frac{f_y}{\sigma} = \frac{235}{222.22} = 1.058$$

This is a very low safety factor — the keel is operating close to yield. Classification societies typically require SF ≥ 1.5 for normal sea conditions.

**Part (d): Lateral Strain**

$$\epsilon_{lateral} = -\nu \cdot \epsilon_{long} = -(0.30)(1.111 \times 10^{-3}) = -3.333 \times 10^{-4}$$

The negative sign indicates **contraction** (plate narrows while being stretched).

**Summary of Results:**
| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Stress | 222.22 MPa |
| Strain (long) | 1.111 × 10⁻³ |
| Safety factor | 1.058 |
| Lateral strain | -3.33 × 10⁻⁴ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Stress-Strain Test Apparatus and Identification of Mechanical Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Universal Testing Machine Test on Marine Steel Specimen] --> B[Apply Uniaxial Tensile Load F]
    B --> C[Record Force F and Elongation ΔL]
    C --> D[Compute Engineering Stress sigma and Engineering Strain epsilon]
    D --> E{Identify Key Points on Curve}
    E --> F1[Point P: Proportional Limit sigma_p]
    E --> F2[Point Y: Yield Strength sigma_y]
    E --> F3[Point U: Ultimate Tensile Strength sigma_u]
    E --> F4[Point F: Fracture Point sigma_f]
    F1 --> G[Region 1: Elastic Linear - Modulus E = sigma over epsilon]
    F2 --> H[Region 2: Plastic - Modulus of Resilience U_r = sigma_y squared over 2E]
    F3 --> I[Region 3: Strain Hardening - True Stress sigma_T]
    F4 --> J[Region 4: Necking - Ductility percentEL and percentRA]
    G --> K[Derive: G = E over 2 times 1 plus nu]
    G --> L[Derive: K = E over 3 times 1 minus 2nu]
    H --> M[Derive: Modulus of Toughness U_t = Integral sigma de]
    I --> N[Apply: True Stress sigma_T = sigma times 1 plus epsilon]
    J --> O[Apply: percentEL = Lf minus L0 over L0 times 100]
    K --> P[Output: Mechanical Properties Dataset for Fishing Vessel Material Selection]
    L --> P
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchical Classification of Mechanical Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Mechanical Properties of Marine Materials] --> A1[Elastic Properties]
    ROOT --> A2[Plastic Properties]
    ROOT --> A3[Strength Properties]
    ROOT --> A4[Energy Properties]
    ROOT --> A5[Surface Properties]
    ROOT --> A6[Time-Temperature Properties]
    ROOT --> A7[Special Marine Properties]

    A1 --> A1a[Young Modulus E]
    A1 --> A1b[Shear Modulus G]
    A1 --> A1c[Bulk Modulus K]
    A1 --> A1d[Poisson Ratio nu]

    A2 --> A2a[Yield Strength sigma_y]
    A2 --> A2b[Ultimate Tensile sigma_u]
    A2 --> A2c[Ductility percentEL]
    A2 --> A2d[Strain Hardening E_sh]

    A3 --> A3a[Yield Criteria Tresca]
    A3 --> A3b[Yield Criteria von Mises]
    A3 --> A3c[Fatigue Limit sigma_f]

    A4 --> A4a[Modulus of Resilience U_r]
    A4 --> A4b[Modulus of Toughness U_t]

    A5 --> A5a[Brinell Hardness HB]
    A5 --> A5b[Vickers Hardness HV]
    A5 --> A5c[Rockwell HRB HRC]
    A5 --> A5d[Wear Resistance]

    A6 --> A6a[Creep Rate epsilon dot]
    A6 --> A6b[Stress Relaxation]
    A6 --> A6c[Thermal Stress sigma_th]

    A7 --> A7a[Charpy Impact KV]
    A7 --> A7b[DBTT]
    A7 --> A7c[Corrosion Rate mm per year]
    A7 --> A7d[Fatigue S-N Curve]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Decision Flow for Material Selection in a Fishing Vessel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: New Fishing Vessel Design Brief] --> Q1{Vessel Length L?}
    Q1 -->|Under 12 m| M1[Select FRP with Vinylester or Polyester Resin]
    Q1 -->|12 to 24 m| M2{Operating Sea?}
    Q1 -->|Over 24 m| M3[Select Mild Steel Grade A ASTM A131]
    Q2 -->|Cold Seas below 0 C| M4[Select Grade D or E Ship Steel]
    Q2 -->|Tropical Seas| M5[Select Grade A Ship Steel or Aluminum 5083]
    M1 --> T1[Verify Mechanical Properties]
    M2 --> Q2
    M3 --> T1
    M4 --> T1
    M5 --> T1
    T1 --> T2[Check Yield Strength f_y]
    T1 --> T3[Check Charpy KV at Service T]
    T1 --> T4[Check Corrosion Rate]
    T1 --> T5[Check Weldability CE Carbon Equivalent]
    T2 --> V[Final Material Approved]
    T3 --> V
    T4 --> V
    T5 --> V
```

### 4.4 Schematic Description (Text-Based) — Stress-Strain Diagram of Marine Steel

Since a true stress-strain curve requires a complex coordinate graph, here is a textual schematic representation:

```
Stress σ (MPa)
    ^
    |                              ____ U (Ultimate, 400 MPa)
    |                          ___/
    |                      ___/
    |                  ____/ ← Strain hardening region
    |               __/
    |            __/
    |     Y ___/ ← Yield Point (235 MPa for Grade A)
    |     /|
    |    / |   ← Plastic region begins
    |   /  |
    |  /   |
    | /    |
    |/_____|________________________→ Strain ε (mm/mm)
    P  ε_y  ε_u        ε_f
    ↑
    Proportional
    Limit (≈ 200 MPa)
    
    Elastic region (linear)        Plastic region     Fracture
    
    Slope in elastic region = E = 200 GPa
    Slope in plastic region = E_sh ≈ 5 GPa (strain hardening)
    Area under elastic region = U_r = 0.138 MJ/m³
    Area under entire curve = U_t ≈ 200 MJ/m³
    ε_y ≈ 0.001175 (yield strain)
    ε_u ≈ 0.15 (ultimate strain)
    ε_f ≈ 0.30 (fracture strain)
    %EL ≈ 22% (ductility for Grade A)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (Module Internal – KTU Pattern)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Sample Model]**
Define the following with respect to materials used in fishing vessel construction:
(i) Modulus of Elasticity
(ii) Poisson's Ratio
(iii) Modulus of Resilience

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Modulus of Elasticity ($E$):** Also called **Young's Modulus**, it is the slope of the linear portion of the engineering stress-strain curve in uniaxial loading. It quantifies a material's resistance to elastic deformation.

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\epsilon}$$

Units: **Pa** (or GPa). For mild shipbuilding steel, $E = 200$ GPa.

**(ii) Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$):** Defined as the negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain in a uniaxially loaded specimen.

$$\nu = -\frac{\epsilon_{lateral}}{\epsilon_{longitudinal}}$$

For most marine metals, $\nu \approx 0.30$. Dimensionless.

**(iii) Modulus of Resilience ($U_r$):** The strain energy per unit volume stored in a material up to the elastic limit. It equals the area under the linear portion of the stress-strain curve up to yield.

$$U_r = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$$

Units: **J/m³**. For Grade A steel, $U_r \approx 0.138$ MJ/m³. Indicates the energy a material can absorb without permanent deformation — important for shock loading on fishing vessel hulls.

---

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Sample Model]**
Distinguish clearly between **Modulus of Resilience** and **Modulus of Toughness**. Which property is more relevant for evaluating the performance of a fishing trawler's hull plate, and why?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Modulus of Resilience ($U_r$) | Modulus of Toughness ($U_t$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Strain energy stored elastically up to yield | Strain energy absorbed up to fracture |
| Formula | $U_r = \sigma_y^2 / (2E)$ | $U_t = \int_0^{\epsilon_f} \sigma \, d\epsilon$ |
| Region of Curve | Linear elastic portion only | Entire area under stress-strain curve |
| Typical Value (Steel) | 0.138 MJ/m³ | ~200 MJ/m³ |
| Indicators | Shock resistance (no permanent damage) | Resistance to fracture |

**Relevance for Fishing Trawler Hull Plate:**

**Modulus of Toughness ($U_t$)** is more relevant because a fishing trawler's hull plate must withstand not only normal wave loading (elastic regime) but also extreme events like collision with submerged objects, grounding, accidental overload, and slamming. These events drive the material into the plastic regime. A high $U_t$ ensures the plate absorbs substantial energy before fracturing — giving the crew time to abandon ship or limit flooding.

The Modulus of Resilience is useful only for routine shock loads (e.g., minor berthing impacts).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

#### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Sample Model — Module 4 Internal Choice Question A]**

(a) **(7 Marks)** With a neat stress-strain diagram for a typical mild shipbuilding steel (Grade A), explain the significance of: Proportional Limit, Yield Strength, Ultimate Tensile Strength, and Fracture Point. State the standard numerical values of these properties for Grade A ship steel.

(b) **(7 Marks)** A circular hull plate support bracket in a fishing vessel is made of Grade A steel ($\sigma_y = 235$ MPa, $E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.30$). It is subjected to a tensile load of 50 kN. The cross-section is circular with a diameter of 25 mm. Calculate:
   (i) The tensile stress induced.
   (ii) The longitudinal strain.
   (iii) The lateral strain.
   (iv) The Shear Modulus $G$ using the relationship between $E$, $G$, and $\nu$.

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**Part (a) — Stress-Strain Diagram Explanation**

(Refer to the schematic in Section 4.4 above.)

| Point | Definition | Grade A Ship Steel Value |
|---|---|---|
| **P — Proportional Limit** | The maximum stress up to which stress is linearly proportional to strain (Hooke's Law valid) | ≈ 200 MPa |
| **Y — Yield Strength ($f_y$)** | The stress at which material begins to deform plastically (permanent deformation) without any increase in load | **235 MPa** (minimum per ASTM A131) |
| **U — Ultimate Tensile Strength ($f_u$)** | The maximum stress the material can withstand; beyond this, necking begins | **400–490 MPa** |
| **F — Fracture Point** | The stress at which the specimen physically separates into two pieces | Lower than $f_u$ due to necking |

**Significance for Fishing Vessel Design:**
- **Below Proportional Limit**: Hull operates safely under all routine wave loads.
- **At Yield Strength**: Permanent deformation begins — hull plating becomes wavy, frames kink. This is the **classification society allowable limit** divided by a safety factor.
- **At Ultimate Strength**: Hull has reached its absolute load-bearing capacity. Beyond this, failure is imminent.
- **At Fracture**: Hull breaches — flooding, sinking, possible loss of vessel.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**(i) Tensile Stress**

Cross-sectional area of a circular rod of diameter $d = 25$ mm:

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (25)^2}{4} = \frac{625\pi}{4} = 490.87 \text{ mm}^2 = 4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

Tensile stress:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{4.9087 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\sigma = 1.0186 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = 101.86 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Longitudinal Strain**

Using Hooke's Law:

$$\epsilon_{long} = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{101.86 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9}$$

$$\epsilon_{long} = 5.093 \times 10^{-4} = 0.05093\%$$

**(iii) Lateral Strain**

$$\epsilon_{lateral} = -\nu \cdot \epsilon_{long} = -(0.30)(5.093 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$\epsilon_{lateral} = -1.528 \times 10^{-4} = -0.01528\%$$

**(iv) Shear Modulus**

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{200}{2(1 + 0.30)} = \frac{200}{2.60}$$

$$\boxed{G = 76.92 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Verification:** The induced stress (101.86 MPa) is well below $f_y$ (235 MPa). Safety factor:

$$\text{SF} = \frac{235}{101.86} = 2.307$$

This is acceptable per classification society rules (SF ≥ 1.5–2.0 for normal seagoing service). ✓

---

#### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Sample Model — Module 4 Internal Choice Question B]**

(a) **(7 Marks)** Define **Fatigue Failure**. With the help of an S-N curve, explain the difference between ferrous (steel) and non-ferrous (aluminum) materials with respect to fatigue limit. Discuss the relevance of fatigue analysis for fishing vessels operating in the Arabian Sea.

(b) **(7 Marks)** A fishing trawler's steel hull plate is subjected to cyclic wave loading. The mean stress is $\sigma_m = 80$ MPa and the alternating stress amplitude is $\sigma_a = 60$ MPa. The ultimate tensile strength is $f_u = 410$ MPa and the fully-reversed fatigue limit is $\sigma'_f = 210$ MPa. Using the **Goodman criterion**, determine the factor of safety against fatigue failure. Comment on whether the design is safe.

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**Part (a) — Fatigue Failure**

**Definition:** Fatigue failure is the progressive and localized structural damage caused by cyclic loading at stress levels BELOW the yield strength of the material. It occurs in three stages:
1. **Crack Initiation** — at a microscopic defect, weld toe, or surface scratch.
2. **Crack Propagation** — the crack grows incrementally with each load cycle.
3. **Final Fracture** — sudden catastrophic failure when the remaining cross-section can no longer support the load.

**S-N Curve Comparison (Steel vs. Aluminum):**

```
Stress S
    ^
    |  * Steel Fatigue Curve:
    |   *  Decreasing, flattens at:
    |     *---------___  ← σ'_f (Endurance Limit, ~210 MPa for steel)
    |     |
    |     |  * Aluminum Fatigue Curve:
    |     |  * Continuously decreasing, NO knee
    |     |   \
    |     |    \
    |     |     \  (no true endurance limit)
    |_____|______\______________→ Log N (cycles)
```

**Difference:**
- **Steel:** Has a true **endurance limit** $\sigma'_f$ (typically $0.4$ to $0.5 f_u$). Below this stress, the material has theoretically infinite life.
- **Aluminum:** Has NO true endurance limit. The S-N curve keeps decreasing. A "fatigue strength" is defined at a specific cycle count (usually $N = 10^7$ or $5 \times 10^8$).

**Relevance to Fishing Vessels in Arabian Sea:**
- Fishing trawlers experience **billions of wave-induced stress cycles** during their 25-year service life.
- The Arabian Sea has significant seasonal wave activity (pre-monsoon, monsoon, post-monsoon) producing irregular but cyclic loads.
- Welded joints are prime sites for fatigue crack initiation; fishing vessels have many welded plate-frame intersections.
- DNV and ABS fatigue rules per **DNVGL-RP-C203** are mandatory for classification.

**Part (b) — Goodman Fatigue Analysis**

**Goodman's Equation:**

$$\sigma_a = \sigma'_f \left[1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{f_u}\right]$$

The allowable alternating stress for the given mean stress is:

$$\sigma_{a,allow} = 210 \left[1 - \frac{80}{410}\right] = 210 \left[1 - 0.1951\right] = 210 \times 0.8049$$

$$\sigma_{a,allow} = 169.03 \text{ MPa}$$

**Factor of Safety (FoS):**

$$\text{FoS} = \frac{\sigma_{a,allow}}{\sigma_{a,applied}} = \frac{169.03}{60}$$

$$\boxed{\text{FoS} = 2.817}$$

**Safety Commentary:**

A FoS of 2.817 indicates the hull plate has substantial margin against fatigue failure. Per DNVGL-RP-C203 and ABS rules, the design is **SAFE** for long-term cyclic service, as typical acceptable FoS ≥ 1.5–2.0 for ship structural details.

**Additional maximum and minimum stresses:**

$$\sigma_{max} = \sigma_m + \sigma_a = 80 + 60 = 140 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{min} = \sigma_m - \sigma_a = 80 - 60 = 20 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = 140 \text{ MPa} < f_y = 235 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(No static yield — OK)}$$

$$\text{Stress Ratio: } R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max} = 20/140 = 0.143 \quad \text{(Tension-tension cycle, common for wave-loaded hull)}$$

**Design Verdict:** SAFE for fatigue with comfortable margin.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Exams (Module 4 – Mechanical Properties):**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert mm² → m² when calculating stress. Use $1 \text{ mm}^2 = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$ and stress comes out in Pa. NEVER report stress in N/mm² without explicitly stating "MPa".
>
> 2. **Using $f_u$ instead of $f_y$ for Allowable Stress (–3 marks):** The Working Stress Design (WSD) approach uses $\sigma_{allow} = f_y / \text{SF}$, NOT $f_u$. Many students write "allowable stress = $f_u / \text{SF}$" — wrong.
>
> 3. **Confusing Engineering and True Stress (–2 marks):** If a question says "after necking begins", you MUST use true stress. For elastic region only, use engineering stress.
>
> 4. **Misapplying Hooke's Law in 2D/3D (–3 marks):** When stress is applied in two directions, $\epsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \nu \sigma_y]$. Forgetting the Poisson term is a major deduction.
>
> 5. **Ignoring Units in Charpy Test (–1 mark):** Charpy impact energy is in **Joules (J)**, NOT N-m·deg. State the testing temperature explicitly.
>
> 6. **Confusing Hardness Conversions (–2 marks):** $HB$, $HV$, and $HRC$ are NOT linearly related. Use the standard conversion tables (ASTM E140), not approximations.
>
> 7. **Forgetting Material Density in Specific Strength Calculation (–2 marks):** "Specific strength" = strength / density. For fishing vessel light-weighting (e.g., aluminum vs. steel comparison), the calculation is mandatory.
>
> 8. **Miner's Rule Application (–3 marks):** The partial damage $\sum (n_i / N_i)$ must consider EACH stress level separately, not the average.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS — MUST MEMORIZE for KTU 2024 ESE:**
>
> ✅ **Definitions:**
> - Stress = Force / Area; Strain = Elongation / Original Length.
> - Young's Modulus ($E$) = slope of stress-strain curve in linear elastic region.
> - Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$) = -lateral strain / longitudinal strain (≈ 0.30 for metals).
> - Modulus of Resilience ($U_r$) = $\sigma_y^2 / (2E)$ — elastic energy per unit volume.
> - Modulus of Toughness ($U_t$) = $\int \sigma \, d\epsilon$ — total energy to fracture.
> - Fatigue failure = cyclic-loading failure below yield, in 3 stages.
> - Creep = time-dependent deformation at high temperature.
>
> ✅ **Key Numerical Values (must memorize):**
> - Grade A ship steel: $f_y = 235$ MPa, $f_u = 400$–490 MPa, $E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.30$.
> - Aluminum 5083-H116: $f_y = 215$ MPa, $\rho = 2660$ kg/m³.
> - Charpy V-notch for Grade A: $KV \geq 27$ J at $+20$°C.
> - Steel fatigue limit ≈ $0.5 f_u$.
> - Aluminum has NO true fatigue limit.
> - $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$, $K = E / [3(1-2\nu)]$.
> - Thermal stress $\sigma_{th} = E \alpha \Delta T$.
>
> ✅ **Diagrams to Practice:**
> - Engineering stress-strain curve with all 4 points labeled.
> - True stress-strain curve.
> - S-N curve for steel (showing knee at $10^6$ cycles).
> - S-N curve for aluminum (no knee).
> - Charpy impact curve (Ductile-to-Brittle Transition).
>
> ✅ **Failure Theories:**
> - Tresca (max shear): $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \leq f_y$.
> - von Mises (distortion energy): $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2]/2} \leq f_y$.
>
> ✅ **Cumulative Damage:**
> - Miner's rule: $D = \sum n_i / N_i \leq 1$.
>
> ✅ **Marine-Specific Concerns:**
> - Corrosion allowance (typically 1–3 mm per side for steel hull).
> - Charpy impact at service temperature.
> - Fatigue S-N curves per DNVGL-RP-C203.
> - Weldability via Carbon Equivalent (CE) ≤ 0.45%.
>
> ✅ **Material Selection Quick Rule for Fishing Vessels:**
> - < 12 m: FRP / Wood / Aluminum.
> - 12–24 m: Aluminum or Light Steel.
> - > 24 m: Mild Steel (Grade A) or High Tensile (AH36).
> - Cold seas: Grade D or E steel.
> - Tropical seas: Grade A or Aluminum 5083.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of Hulls of Different Material
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST833 - BASICS OF FISHING VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Comparison of Hulls of Different Material

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hull Material** refers to the structural engineering material used in the construction of the watertight body (hull) of a fishing vessel. The hull must withstand hydrostatic pressure, hydrodynamic loads, slamming forces, bending moments, and corrosive marine environment throughout the vessel's operational life.

In **fishing vessel design**, the choice of hull material fundamentally dictates the vessel's:
- **Displacement-to-weight ratio**
- **Payload capacity (Deadweight)**
- **Operational range and fuel efficiency**
- **Maintenance schedule and lifecycle cost**
- **Regulatory classification** (as per IMO and Indian Register of Shipping standards)

The five principal hull materials used globally and within Indian fishing fleets are:

| # | Material | KTU Designation |
|---|----------|-----------------|
| 1 | Wood | Traditional Hull Material |
| 2 | Steel (Mild/Corten) | Conventional Metallic Hull |
| 3 | Aluminum Alloys | Lightweight Metallic Hull |
| 4 | FRP / GRP (Fiberglass Reinforced Plastic) | Composite Hull |
| 5 | Ferro-cement | Cementitious Hull |
| 6 | Reinforced Concrete | Specialized Hull |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Understanding)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a fishing vessel hull like a boat's "skeleton and skin" combined.** Just as humans build houses from wood (traditional), bricks/concrete (heavy, durable), steel (high-rise), or modern composites (prefab villas) — boat builders choose hull materials based on:
> - **How big the boat is** (size)
> - **How fast it needs to go** (speed)
> - **How salty and harsh the water is** (environment)
> - **How much money the owner has** (budget)
> - **How easily it can be repaired at sea** (practicality)

A small canoe in a Kerala backwater can be **wood or FRP** (light, cheap, easy to repair). A 25-meter trawler operating in the Arabian Sea **must be steel** (strong, durable, handles big waves). A high-speed patrol boat uses **aluminum** (light + strong = speed). A small artisanal canoe in rural Tamil Nadu might be **ferro-cement** (cheap, uses local labor).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants Used in Hull Material Selection (Bolded for KTU Exam Importance):**
>
> - **Density of Seawater:** **ρ_sw = 1.025 t/m³**
> - **Acceleration due to gravity:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - **Specific Gravity of Steel:** **7.85**
> - **Specific Gravity of Aluminum Alloy:** **2.70**
> - **Specific Gravity of Wood (Teak, seasoned):** **0.65 – 0.85**
> - **Specific Gravity of FRP (typical):** **1.45 – 1.60**
> - **Specific Gravity of Ferro-cement (cured):** **2.30 – 2.50**
> - **Specific Gravity of Reinforced Concrete:** **2.40 – 2.50**

The **Lightweight Factor** is often used to compare materials:

$$\text{Lightweight Factor} = \frac{\text{Yield Strength (MPa)}}{\text{Density (kg/m}^3\text{)}}$$

This ratio is crucial because it tells us which material gives the most strength per unit weight — vital for fishing vessels that need to carry more fish (deadweight) without increasing displacement.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Hull Materials — Structural Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Wood (Traditional Material)

**Composition:** Teak, Sal, Deodar, Mahogany, Marine Ply, or country-craft wood (Coconut, Aini, Irul in Indian context).

**Why "Wood" Logic:**
- Wood was humanity's first boat-building material (dating back ~10,000 years).
- Easily carved, shaped, and stitched/carvel-planked.
- Naturally buoyant (lower density than water).
- Absorbs vibration and shock well — comfortable in rough seas.

**How It Performs:**
- **Strength-to-weight ratio:** Moderate.
- **Limitation:** Subject to rot, marine borer attack (Teredo navalis), and drying cracks.
- **Modern use:** Confined to small craft (<15 m) and traditional country boats.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Wood hulls require **annual dry-docking, caulking, and anti-fouling repainting**. Maintenance cost over 20 years often exceeds initial construction cost.

### 2.1.2 Steel (Most Common Industrial Material)

**Composition:** Mild Steel (Grade A, B, D, E per classification societies) or **Cor-ten steel** (high-strength low-alloy, weathering steel).

**Why Steel Logic:**
- **High tensile strength** (yield strength = 235–355 MPa).
- Homogeneous and isotropic — predictable stress behavior.
- Readily weldable using SMAW, GMAW, FCAW processes.
- Globally available; well-established classification rules (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV, BV).

**How It Performs:**
- Excellent for vessels **> 20 m** LOA.
- Requires **cathodic protection + coating system** to resist corrosion.
- Heavier than aluminum or FRP, but cheaper per kg.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Indian Register of Shipping (IRS) Rule:** For steel fishing vessels, the **minimum plate thickness** for shell plating is governed by the formula (simplified):

$$t = \frac{L \cdot \sqrt{d}}{20} + C \quad \text{(in mm)}$$

where **L** = Rule Length (m), **d** = Rule Depth (m), **C** = corrosion allowance (typically 1.5–2.0 mm).

### 2.1.3 Aluminum Alloys (Lightweight Metallic)

**Composition:** Marine grades **5083, 5086, 5454, 6061** (Al-Mg and Al-Mg-Si alloys).

**Why Aluminum Logic:**
- **Density = 2,700 kg/m³** — one-third of steel.
- Excellent corrosion resistance in seawater due to protective oxide layer (Al₂O₃).
- Used for **high-speed craft, patrol vessels, and small fishing boats** where speed and payload matter.

**How It Performs:**
- **Yield strength:** ~215–275 MPa (lower than mild steel).
- Requires special welding (TIG/MIG with Al-Si filler rods).
- **More expensive per kg** than steel (~3–4× cost).
- Vulnerable to **galvanic corrosion** when in contact with steel or copper.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Aluminum hulls MUST be insulated from steel fittings using **neoprene gaskets or zinc anodes**, otherwise rapid galvanic corrosion destroys the hull within 2–3 years.

### 2.1.4 FRP / GRP (Fiber Reinforced Plastic)

**Composition:** Polyester or vinyl-ester resin reinforced with **E-glass fiber** (woven roving + chopped strand mat lay-up).

**Why FRP Logic:**
- **No corrosion** — ideal for saline tropical waters.
- **Low maintenance** (no repainting for 5–7 years).
- **Mold-based construction** allows rapid replication.
- Smooth hull surface reduces frictional resistance by **8–12%** vs. steel.

**How It Performs:**
- **Density:** ~1,500 kg/m³.
- **Tensile strength:** ~200–300 MPa (depends on fiber volume fraction).
- **Limitation:** Poor fire resistance; susceptible to UV degradation if unprotected; repairs require skilled labor.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Indian fishing fleet modernization program (PMMSY scheme) actively promotes **FRP boats for up to 20 m LOA** because of low lifecycle cost.

### 2.1.5 Ferro-Cement (Cementitious Hull)

**Composition:** Portland cement mortar reinforced with **chicken mesh + skeletal steel rods + wire mesh** in multiple layers.

**Why Ferro-Cement Logic:**
- **Very low material cost** — uses cement, sand, and wire mesh.
- Can be built by **semi-skilled labor** with minimal infrastructure.
- Suitable for **artisan fishing vessels < 12 m** in developing coastal communities.

**How It Performs:**
- **Density:** ~2,400 kg/m³ (similar to concrete).
- **Tensile strength:** Only **5–10 MPa** (very low — relies on mesh reinforcement).
- **Crack-prone** if not cured properly.
- Heavy; poor payload efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FAO Recommendation:** Ferro-cement is recommended only for **non-powered or low-powered artisanal craft** in sheltered waters. Not suitable for offshore fishing.

### 2.1.6 Reinforced Concrete (Specialized Use)

**Composition:** Concrete (cement + aggregate + water) reinforced with steel rebars.

**Why Concrete Logic:**
- Used only in **pontoons, floating docks, and barges** — not typical fishing vessels.
- Extremely heavy; brittle under impact loading.
- Not recommended for hulls of operational fishing boats.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas use **SI units** unless specified. Standard KTU convention.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|----------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $\rho_{\text{hull}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{hull}}}{V_{\text{hull}}}$ | Hull density = mass / volume | kg/m³ | Material comparison |
| 2 | $\text{Specific Strength} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\rho}$ | Strength-to-weight ratio | kNm/kg | Material selection efficiency |
| 3 | $\text{Buoyancy Force } F_b = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{\text{sub}}$ | Seawater density × gravity × submerged volume | N (kN) | Hull displacement |
| 4 | $t = \dfrac{L \cdot \sqrt{d}}{20} + C$ | Plate thickness (steel IRS rule) | mm | Shell plating |
| 5 | $t_{Al} = t_{steel} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_{y,steel}}{\sigma_{y,Al}}}$ | Equivalent Al plate thickness | mm | Aluminum hull design |
| 6 | $\text{Lightweight Factor } = \dfrac{1}{\rho_{mat}}$ | Inverse density | m³/kg | Weight-saving analysis |
| 7 | $W_{hull} = \rho_{mat} \cdot A_{surface} \cdot t_{avg}$ | Hull weight = density × surface area × thickness | kg or tonnes | Comparative estimation |
| 8 | $\text{Deadweight Ratio (DWR)} = \dfrac{DWT}{\text{Displacement}}$ | Cargo capacity fraction | dimensionless | Material impact on payload |
| 9 | $t_{FRP} = k \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{L \cdot d}{1000}}$ | Empirical FRP shell thickness | mm | FRP hull rule (approx.) |
| 10 | $\text{LCF} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\rho \cdot g}$ | Length-to-Collapse Factor | m | Material efficiency rating |

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Domain | Application of Hull Material Selection |
|--------|---------------------------------------|
| **Deep-sea Trawlers** (>24 m) | Steel — withstands slamming, high hogging/sagging moments |
| **Tuna Long-liners** (20–30 m) | Steel (dominant) or FRP for upper-end |
| **Purse Seiners** | Steel — high deck load capacity |
| **Speed Boats & Patrol** | Aluminum — speed and weight advantage |
| **Coastal Artisanal Boats** (<10 m) | FRP / Wood / Ferro-cement — cost-effective |
| **Research Vessels** | Steel hull + FRP superstructure (hybrid) |
| **Traditional Country Boats** | Wood — cultural and practical heritage |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hull Weight Estimation for Different Materials

**Problem Statement:** A fishing vessel has a **wetted surface area (WSA)** of $180 \text{ m}^2$ and **average plate thickness** of $8 \text{ mm}$. Estimate the hull weight for five materials: Steel, Aluminum, Wood, FRP, and Ferro-cement.

### Given Data

$$A_{WSA} = 180 \text{ m}^2, \quad t_{avg} = 8 \text{ mm} = 0.008 \text{ m}$$

**Volume of hull plating:**

$$V_{hull} = A_{WSA} \times t_{avg}$$

$$V_{hull} = 180 \times 0.008 = 1.44 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 1 — Steel Hull Weight

$$\rho_{steel} = 7,850 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$W_{steel} = \rho_{steel} \times V_{hull} = 7,850 \times 1.44$$

$$W_{steel} = 11,304 \text{ kg} \approx 11.30 \text{ tonnes}$$

### Step 2 — Aluminum Hull Weight

$$\rho_{Al} = 2,700 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$W_{Al} = 2,700 \times 1.44 = 3,888 \text{ kg} \approx 3.89 \text{ tonnes}$$

### Step 3 — Wood (Teak) Hull Weight

$$\rho_{wood} = 750 \text{ kg/m}^3 \text{ (avg)}$$

$$W_{wood} = 750 \times 1.44 = 1,080 \text{ kg} = 1.08 \text{ tonnes}$$

### Step 4 — FRP Hull Weight

$$\rho_{FRP} = 1,500 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$W_{FRP} = 1,500 \times 1.44 = 2,160 \text{ kg} = 2.16 \text{ tonnes}$$

### Step 5 — Ferro-cement Hull Weight

$$\rho_{fc} = 2,400 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$W_{fc} = 2,400 \times 1.44 = 3,456 \text{ kg} \approx 3.46 \text{ tonnes}$$

### Comparative Table (Analytical Matrix)

| Material | Density (kg/m³) | Hull Weight (t) | Weight Saving vs. Steel | Cost Index (per t) |
|----------|-----------------|------------------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| Steel | 7,850 | 11.30 | — (baseline) | 1.0× |
| Aluminum | 2,700 | 3.89 | 65.6% lighter | 4.0× |
| Wood | 750 | 1.08 | 90.4% lighter | 1.5× (varies) |
| FRP | 1,500 | 2.16 | 80.9% lighter | 2.5× |
| Ferro-cement | 2,400 | 3.46 | 69.4% lighter | 0.4× (cheapest) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** For the same plate thickness, aluminum saves ~65% weight vs. steel, but at ~4× cost. Wood is lightest but limited in size. FRP offers the **best weight-to-cost compromise** for small-medium fishing vessels.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equivalent Plate Thickness for Different Materials

**Concept:** When replacing steel with aluminum or FRP, the **thickness must be increased** to maintain equivalent strength (because yield strength varies).

### Step 1 — Bending Stress Formula (Bernoulli Beam Theory for Plate)

The bending stress in a plate under uniform load is:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$$

For a plate of unit width, the section modulus is:

$$Z = \dfrac{1 \cdot t^2}{6}$$

Setting equal bending strength:

$$\sigma_y = \dfrac{6M}{t^2}$$

### Step 2 — Equating Strength for Two Materials

For Steel and Aluminum under **same bending moment M**:

$$\dfrac{6M}{t_{steel}^2} = \sigma_{y,steel} \quad \text{and} \quad \dfrac{6M}{t_{Al}^2} = \sigma_{y,Al}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Equivalent Aluminum Thickness

$$t_{Al} = t_{steel} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_{y,steel}}{\sigma_{y,Al}}}$$

Substituting values: $\sigma_{y,steel} = 235$ MPa, $\sigma_{y,Al} = 215$ MPa:

$$t_{Al} = 8 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{235}{215}} = 8 \cdot \sqrt{1.0930}$$

$$t_{Al} = 8 \times 1.0455 = 8.36 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 4 — Result Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> Aluminum plate must be **8.36 mm thick** to match the strength of an **8 mm steel plate**. However, the weight saving is still substantial because aluminum density is one-third of steel.

**Weight comparison for equivalent strength:**

$$W_{steel,equiv} = 7850 \times 1.44 = 11.30 \text{ t}$$

$$W_{Al,equiv} = 2700 \times (180 \times 0.00836) = 2700 \times 1.505 = 4.06 \text{ t}$$

**Net weight saving:** $11.30 - 4.06 = 7.24$ tonnes → **64% lighter** even with increased thickness.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comprehensive Hull Material Selection Chart

The following decision matrix summarizes **all critical parameters** for KTU exam preparation:

| Parameter | Wood | Steel | Aluminum | FRP | Ferro-cement |
|-----------|------|-------|----------|-----|--------------|
| **Density (kg/m³)** | 600–900 | 7,850 | 2,700 | 1,400–1,600 | 2,300–2,500 |
| **Yield Strength (MPa)** | 50–80 (parallel to grain) | 235–355 | 125–275 | 200–300 | 5–10 |
| **Young's Modulus (GPa)** | 9–12 | 210 | 70 | 15–25 | 25–30 |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | Poor (rots) | Moderate | Excellent (oxide layer) | Excellent | Good (if sealed) |
| **Maintenance Frequency** | Annual | 2–3 years | 3–5 years | 5–7 years | Annual |
| **Construction Cost** | Medium | Low | High | Medium-High | Very Low |
| **Skilled Labor Required** | High | Medium | High | Medium | Low |
| **Repair at Sea** | Easy | Moderate | Difficult | Moderate-Easy | Difficult |
| **Max Practical LOA (m)** | ~20 | >100 | ~40 | ~30 | ~15 |
| **Fire Resistance** | Poor | Excellent | Good | Poor | Excellent |
| **Recommended Use** | Artisanal boats | Trawlers, seiners | Speed boats | Modern small craft | Rural canoes |

## 3.4 Selection Decision Logic (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1:** Determine vessel **Length Overall (LOA)**.
- LOA < 12 m → Consider Wood / FRP / Ferro-cement.
- LOA 12–25 m → FRP, Steel, or Aluminum.
- LOA > 25 m → Steel (mandatory for ocean-going).

**Step 2:** Determine **Operating Environment**.
- Inland/sheltered → Any material.
- Coastal → Steel, Aluminum, FRP.
- Offshore deep-sea → Steel only (for >20 m).

**Step 3:** Determine **Speed Requirement**.
- <8 knots → Steel, Wood, FRP acceptable.
- 8–20 knots → Aluminum, FRP.
- >20 knots → Aluminum (only viable lightweight option).

**Step 4:** Evaluate **Budget & Maintenance Capacity**.
- Low budget, low maintenance → Steel.
- Medium budget, low maintenance → FRP.
- High budget, performance critical → Aluminum.
